title: Professor
bio: Dmitry Kramkov's research focuses on mathematical finance and stochastic processes, with particular emphasis on equilibrium-based models and their mathematical foundations. His recent work includes continuous-time price impact models, conditions for existence of Arrow-Debreu and Radner equilibria, and analysis of backward stochastic differential equations in BMO spaces.

name: Martin Larsson
title: Professor
bio: Martin Larsson's core research area is mathematical finance and related topics in stochastic analysis and probability. His work centers on the theory and applications of finite- and infinite-dimensional affine and polynomial processes, stochastic convolution equations, and stochastic portfolio theory.

name: Giovanni Leoni
title: Professor
bio: Giovanni Leoni's research focuses on calculus of variations, partial differential equations, and geometric measure theory with applications to continuum mechanics, materials science, and imaging. His recent work includes variational problems involving material defects, epitaxial growth, phase field models, and water waves.

name: Po-Shen Loh
title: Professor
bio: Po-Shen's research explores the intersection of combinatorics, probability, and computer science, examining how randomness manifests in the construction of combinatorial systems and serves as a proof technique for deterministic systems. He is the Academic Director of the United States Mathematical Olympiad Program and serves as the national lead coach for the United States in international mathematical competitions.

name: Sergey Nadtochiy
title: Professor
bio: Sergey Nadtochiy is a Professor of Mathematical Sciences whose research focuses on stochastic processes and their applications to mathematical finance. His work includes the development of rigorous mathematical frameworks for understanding complex financial systems and related probabilistic phenomena.

name: Wesley Pegden
title: Professor
bio: Wesley Pegden's research focuses on discrete mathematics, including probabilistic combinatorics, combinatorial game theory, graph theory, and discrete geometry. His recent work centers on the Abelian sandpile, a deterministic diffusion process that produces striking fractal limits, and its connection to Apollonian circle packings through integer superharmonic functions.

name: Javier Peña
title: Professor of Operations Research
bio: Javier Peña is a Professor of Operations Research in the Department of Mathematical Sciences with a courtesy appointment in the Tepper School of Business. His research focuses on optimization methods, convex optimization, and the development of efficient algorithms for solving large-scale mathematical programs.

name: Agoston Pisztora
title: Associate Professor
bio: Agoston Pisztora's research focuses on probabilistic analysis of random fields, disordered systems, and phase transitions, with the goal of explaining macroscopic phenomena through microscopic probabilistic analysis. His work explores how random systems exhibit deterministic behavior through self-averaging and how randomness can be retained or created at macroscopic levels in critical systems.

name: Matthew Rosenzweig
title: Assistant Professor
bio: Matthew Rosenzweig's research lies at the intersection of mathematical physics, nonlinear partial differential equations, and probability, with recent focus on nonlinear dispersive, fluid, and kinetic equations. He studies the mathematical foundations of these equations through scaling limits derived from underlying physical systems such as classical and quantum particle or wave systems.

name: Ernest Schimmerling
title: Professor
bio: Ernest Schimmerling is a set theorist whose research focuses on the hierarchy of large cardinal axioms and techniques for building inner models that generalize Gödel's Constructible Universe. His work explores connections between inner model theory, descriptive set theory, infinitary combinatorics, cardinal arithmetic, and forcing.

name: Mykhaylo Shkolnikov
title: Professor
bio: Mykhaylo Shkolnikov studies interacting particle systems arising in mathematical finance, mathematical physics, and neuroscience using tools from stochastic analysis and PDE/SPDE. His broader interests include random operators, integrable probability, models of random growth, concentration of measure, large deviations, and probabilistic approaches to PDEs.

name: Dejan Slepčev
title: Professor
bio: Dejan Slepčev's research lies primarily in applied analysis, using partial differential equations, calculus of variations, optimal transportation, and computational tools to address applied problems, particularly in data science and machine learning. His work encompasses variational and PDE-based problems on random data samples, collective behavior of interacting particle systems, and energy-driven systems that govern pattern formation.

name: Richard Statman
title: Professor
bio: Richard Statman studies the deep structure of pure lambda calculus with and without types, exploring the rich mathematical foundations of computation rules and functional programming. His research spans typed lambda calculus, evaluation strategies, combinators, computability, functional equations, and connections to other branches of mathematics such as semigroup theory.

name: Prasad Tetali
title: Alexander M. Knaster Professor, Department Head
bio: Prasad Tetali's research focuses on discrete mathematics, probability, and theoretical computing, with particular expertise in Markov chains, isoperimetry, functional analysis, combinatorics, and algorithms. His work spans the mathematical aspects of mixing times in Markov chains, sampling and counting algorithms, and functional inequalities on graphs.

name: Ian Tice
title: Professor
bio: Ian Tice studies the analysis of nonlinear partial differential equations, particularly those arising in physics such as interfacial fluid mechanics and superconductivity models. His work focuses on understanding the behavior of singularities in these physical systems, including surfaces of discontinuity in fluids and point singularities (vortices) in superconductors.

name: Konstantin Tikhomirov
title: Associate Professor
bio: Konstantin Tikhomirov is an Associate Professor of Mathematical Sciences whose research focuses on probability theory, random matrices, and high-dimensional geometry. His work explores the connections between convex geometry and probabilistic methods in understanding complex mathematical structures.

name: Tomasz Tkocz
title: Associate Professor
bio: Tomasz Tkocz's research focuses on analysis, probability, and convex geometry with emphasis on high-dimensional phenomena and inequalities in information theory. His work spans Gaussian measures, entropy power inequalities, and random matrix theory.

name: Noel J. Walkington
title: Professor
bio: Noel Walkington develops and analyzes numerical algorithms for approximating partial differential equations, with expertise spanning multiphase porous flows, viscoelastic fluids, and liquid crystal dynamics. He works at the interface of mechanical engineering and mathematics, applying computational methods to complex physical systems.

name: Tim Flaherty
title: Associate Teaching Professor
bio: Tim Flaherty is an Associate Teaching Professor focused on teaching and academic development of calculus, vector calculus, and matrix algebra courses, with implementation of online tools. His research interests involve wavelets, fractals, image compression, and tilings, with recent work on Haar-type multitiles in multiple dimensions and their applications to image and data compression.

name: Irina Gheorghiciuc
title: Teaching Professor
bio: Irina Gheorghiciuc's research focuses on algebraic and enumerative combinatorics, particularly combinatorics on words, q-analogs, and lattice path statistics. She is currently working on problems related to Super Catalan Numbers and Polynomials.

name: David Handron
title: Associate Teaching Professor
bio: David Handron studies Morse theory and its applications, particularly focusing on modified gradient vector fields on manifolds with corners and their use in analyzing billiard path problems. He is also developing innovative curriculum approaches for differential equations that integrate computer technology with analytical and qualitative techniques.

name: Jay Howell
title: Teaching Professor
bio: Prof. Howell's research focuses on numerical approximation of PDEs, particularly the theoretical underpinnings of finite element methods and their application to fluid and structure dynamics. He is also interested in applications of mathematics in the sciences, having collaborated on projects ranging from solubility parameters to nanostructures.

name: Gregory Johnson
title: Associate Teaching Professor
bio: Gregory Johnson is a teaching professor whose research focuses on mathematical logic, particularly model theory with an emphasis on abstract elementary classes, and algebraic number theory. He is dedicated to mentoring undergraduate students in research projects, including recent work extending Giuga's conjecture to rings of algebraic integers.

name: John Mackey
title: Teaching Professor
bio: John Mackey studies elusive mathematical objects with special properties, particularly in combinatorics and graph theory, including problems related to Ramsey theory and geometric tiling. His work involves both theoretical research and significant contributions to mathematics education, curriculum development, and outreach to students and the public.

name: David Offner
title: Associate Teaching Professor
bio: David Offner studies packing and decomposition problems, polychromatic coloring problems, and pursuit-evasion games on graphs within combinatorics. He is particularly interested in problems that are accessible to undergraduates and actively mentors undergraduate researchers.

name: Manuel Blum
title: University Professor Emeritus
bio: Manuel Blum is a pioneering computer scientist whose work spans cryptography, complexity theory, and interactive protocols. His research includes foundational contributions to zero-knowledge proofs, program verification, and human-computer authentication systems like CAPTCHAs.

name: Deborah Brandon
title: Associate Teaching Professor Emeritus
bio: Her research focuses on partial differential equations, integrodifferential equations, and nonconvex variational problems with applications in continuum mechanics, including studies of phase transitions and ferromagnetic materials. She has also made significant contributions to mathematical modeling of heat flow and viscoelastic fluids.

name: John P. Lehoczky
title: Thomas Lord University Professor of Statistics Emeritus
bio: John P. Lehoczky's research focuses on stochastic processes and their applications in mathematical finance, computer systems, and manufacturing. His work involves studying optimal consumption and investment policies, analyzing manufacturing systems, and developing scheduling algorithms for real-time computer systems using techniques from martingale theory and Itô calculus.

name: Roy Nicolaides
title: Professor Emeritus
bio: Roy Nicolaides develops accurate computational methods for extracting information from mathematical models using partial differential equations, with applications in electrodynamics, fluid mechanics, materials science, and mathematical finance. His work combines practical algorithm development with theoretical analysis of convergence rates and complexity estimates across diverse computational methods.

name: David R. Owen
title: Professor Emeritus
bio: David R. Owen's research concerns mathematical models for the behavior of continuous bodies, with particular focus on multiscale geometry and deformations that describe geometric changes at microscopic and macroscopic scales. His work extends the foundations of continuum mechanics to include bodies with microstructure such as liquid crystals, defective crystals, granular materials, and mixtures, and has contributed important theorems on uniqueness and continuous dependence in elastic-plastic systems.

name: Robert Pego
title: Professor Emeritus
bio: Robert Pego's research focuses on nonlinear dynamics in partial differential equations, particularly coherent structures and nonlinear waves, including universal scaling behavior in clustering and coarsening phenomena. His work spans solitary wave stability in water wave equations, pressure boundary conditions in Navier-Stokes equations, and applications to coagulation-fragmentation models and animal group dynamics.

name: Jack Schaeffer
title: Professor Emeritus
bio: Jack Schaeffer's research focuses on partial differential equations, particularly kinetic models of plasma described by the Vlasov equation coupled with field equations like Poisson's or Maxwell's equations. His work investigates global existence of smooth solutions, asymptotic behavior, numerical methods, and steady-state solutions for these coupled systems.

name: Robert F. Sekerka
title: University Professor Emeritus
bio: Robert Sekerka's research is interdisciplinary, focusing on theoretical problems in materials science that lead to challenging problems in physics and mathematics, particularly phase transformations and free boundary problems. His work involves linear and non-linear stability analysis, phase field modeling, and the study of crystal shape evolution and dendritic growth patterns.

name: Steven E. Shreve
title: University Professor Emeritus
bio: Steven Shreve's research spans financial mathematics and queueing systems, with expertise in stochastic calculus and continuous-time models for derivative securities, utility maximization, and transaction costs. His recent work combines these areas through diffusion approximations for limit-order books that govern trading on electronic exchanges.

name: Shlomo Ta'asan
title: Professor Emeritus
bio: Shlomo Ta'asan's research focuses on mathematical modeling and simulation of problems in materials science and systems biology, with particular emphasis on grain growth, texture evolution, and grain boundary properties. He also investigates system-level biological problems related to immune response and physiological systems, especially the circulatory system, with applications to understanding failure mechanisms in shock and developing real-time monitoring systems.

name: Luc C. Tartar
title: University Professor of Mathematics, Emeritus
bio: Luc Tartar develops mathematical tools for studying oscillating solutions of nonlinear partial differential equations in continuum mechanics, with applications to understanding macroscopic behavior from microscopic phenomena. His research encompasses homogenization theory, relaxation in nonconvex optimization, hyperbolic conservation laws, and applications to fluid turbulence and quantum mechanics.

name: Russell C. Walker
title: Teaching Professor Emeritus
bio: Russell C. Walker is a Teaching Professor Emeritus whose main interests are in teaching and mathematical exposition, particularly in calculus and optimization courses. He has authored a widely-used textbook on mathematical programming for business majors that has been adopted at multiple universities.

name: William O. Williams
title: Professor Emeritus
bio: William O. Williams studies the formulation and analysis of models in mechanics and thermodynamics, with particular focus on constraint-driven systems such as Tchebychev nets and tensegrity structures. His research spans from structural mechanics to mathematical models of biological systems, including muscle fiber contraction and the mechanics of constrained three-dimensional bodies.

name: Peter B. Andrews
title: Professor
bio: Peter B. Andrews was a Mathematics Professor at Carnegie Mellon University for 49 years who developed the mathematical logic Q0 and created the TPS automated theorem prover. His research combined rigorous mathematical logic with practical educational tools, including the ETPS system designed to help students learn logic through interactive proofs.

name: Walter Noll
title: Professor
bio: Walter Noll was best known for his work in thermodynamics and continuum mechanics, particularly for developing the Coleman-Noll procedure which interprets the Second Law of Thermodynamics and constrains possible material properties. He co-authored the foundational text 'The Non-Linear Field Theories of Mechanics,' which became the standard reference work in the field.

name: John Anderson
title: University Professor / Richard King Mellon Professor of Psychology and Computer Science
bio: John Anderson is a University Professor studying computational approaches to understanding human cognition, learning, and reasoning through behavioral methods and non-invasive brain monitoring techniques. His research integrates psychology and computer science to develop mathematical models of cognitive processes.

name: Alison Barth
title: Maxwell H. and Gloria C. Connan Professor in the Life Sciences
bio: Alison Barth is a professor who studies the characterization of neural circuits and computational neuroscience, employing mathematical and statistical methods to understand brain function. Her research encompasses learning and memory, developmental processes, and sensory perception across multiple levels of neural organization.

name: Marlene Behrmann
title: Thomas S. Baker University Professor of Psychology and Cognitive Neuroscience, Emeritus
bio: Marlene Behrmann is a cognitive neuroscientist studying how the brain processes visual information, language, and spatial attention using behavioral, neural imaging, and computational methods. Her research spans developmental processes, perceptual disorders, and the neural mechanisms underlying attention and reading.

name: Christina Bjorndahl
title: Assistant Teaching Professor
bio: Christina Bjorndahl is an Assistant Teaching Professor of Philosophy whose research focuses on the intersection of phonetics and phonology, examining how sound systems are represented and organized in language. Her work investigates segment inventories, distinctive features, and approaches to linguistic pedagogy.

name: Jessica Cantlon
title: Associate Professor
bio: Jessica Cantlon is Ronald J. and Mary Ann Zdrojkowski Professor of Developmental Neuroscience. Her research investigates how the brain develops and processes cognitive abilities across the lifespan.

name: Maysam Chamanzar
title: Assistant Professor
bio: Maysam Chamanzar is an Assistant Professor in Electrical and Computer Engineering with core faculty status at the Neuroscience Institute. His research focuses on neural engineering technologies and computational methods for non-invasive brain monitoring and analysis.

name: Steve Chase
title: Professor
department: Biomedical Engineering, Electrical and Computer Engineering, Neuroscience Institute
bio: Steve Chase is a Professor studying the characterization of neural circuits and computational neuroscience, with expertise in neural engineering and technology. His research combines mathematical and statistical methods with physiological techniques to understand sensation, perception, and systems-level brain function.

name: Tzahi Cohen-Karni
title: Associate Professor
department: Biomedical Engineering and Materials Science & Engineering
bio: Tzahi Cohen-Karni develops advanced bioelectronic interfaces and nanomaterial-based devices for neural recording and stimulation. His research combines materials science and biomedical engineering to create high-resolution brain-computer interfaces and tools for understanding neural activity.

name: David Creswell
title: William S. Dietrich II Professor in Psychology and Neuroscience
bio: David Creswell's research focuses on understanding what makes people resilient under stress through community intervention studies, laboratory studies, and neuroimaging to examine how stress management strategies alter coping and stress resilience. He investigates how mindfulness meditation training, self-affirmation, and cognitive reappraisal impact the brain, stress physiology, and disease outcomes in at-risk populations.

name: Jenelle Feather
title: Assistant Professor
bio: Jenelle Feather is an Assistant Professor whose research focuses on understanding neural mechanisms of auditory perception through computational approaches. Her work combines neuroscience with machine learning to investigate how the brain processes sound and learns from acoustic signals.

name: Gary Fedder
title: Howard M. Wilkoff Professor
department: Electrical and Computer Engineering and Robotics Institute
bio: Gary Fedder is a professor specializing in microelectromechanical systems (MEMS) and their integration with electronics and robotics. His research focuses on designing and fabricating miniaturized sensors and actuators for advanced robotic and computational applications.

name: Christopher Genovese
title: Professor
bio: Christopher Genovese is a Professor of Statistics and Department Head at Carnegie Mellon University's Neuroscience Institute. His research focuses on developing statistical methods and computational approaches for analyzing complex neuroscience data.

name: Aryn Gittis
title: Associate Professor
bio: Aryn Gittis is an Associate Professor studying the neural circuits underlying motor control and learning, using a combination of behavioral, physiological, and computational approaches. Her research investigates how neural systems encode and process information to generate adaptive motor behaviors and how dysfunction in these circuits contributes to movement disorders.

name: Darcy Griffin
title: Special Faculty Researcher
bio: Darcy Griffin studies skilled voluntary movements and signaling through the corticomotoneuronal system. Their research focuses on understanding the neural mechanisms underlying motor control and coordination.

name: Pulkit Grover
title: Angel Jordan Associate Professor
bio: Pulkit Grover is an Angel Jordan Associate Professor of Electrical and Computer Engineering with core faculty membership in the Neuroscience Institute. His research spans computational neuroscience, neural engineering, and non-invasive brain monitoring to understand neural circuits underlying sensation, perception, motor control, and learning.

name: Bin He
title: Trustee Professor
department: Biomedical Engineering, Electrical and Computer Engineering, Neuroscience Institute
bio: Bin He is a Trustee Professor working on neural engineering, non-invasive brain monitoring, and motor control systems. His research combines methods development and technology innovation to advance understanding of brain function and neural mechanisms.

name: Martial Hebert
title: Dean and Professor
department: School of Computer Science and Robotics Institute
bio: Martial Hebert is Dean and Professor at Carnegie Mellon University's School of Computer Science, leading research in computer vision and robotics. His work focuses on perception systems for autonomous robots and intelligent machines using machine learning and 3D scene understanding.

name: Laurie Heller
title: Teaching Professor
bio: Laurie Heller studies auditory perception and spatial hearing, examining how the brain processes sound location and complex acoustic environments. Her research combines psychoacoustic experiments with neuroscience approaches to understand the neural mechanisms underlying auditory perception.

name: Maggie Henderson
title: Assistant Professor
bio: Maggie Henderson studies human visual perception to understand how the brain computes meaningful information from noisy visual inputs and adapts representations based on environmental statistics and behavioral goals. She employs experimental techniques including fMRI and behavioral studies alongside computational approaches such as neural network modeling and data analysis.

name: Marcel Just
title: D. O. Hebb University Professor and Director of CCBI
bio: Marcel Just is a leading cognitive neuroscientist who uses brain imaging techniques to understand how the brain processes language, reasoning, and other cognitive functions. His research combines neuroimaging data with computational modeling to reveal the neural mechanisms underlying human cognition.

name: Jana Kainerstorfer
title: Associate Professor
department: Biomedical Engineering, Electrical and Computer Engineering, Neuroscience Institute
bio: Jana Kainerstorfer is an Associate Professor developing non-invasive brain monitoring technologies and computational methods for understanding neural activity. Her research spans cognitive neuroscience, neural engineering, and the mathematical/statistical analysis of physiological signals.

name: Robert Kass
title: Maurice Falk Professor of Statistics and Computational Neuroscience
bio: Robert Kass is the Maurice Falk Professor of Statistics and Computational Neuroscience, developing mathematical and statistical methods for understanding neural circuits and brain function. His research spans computational approaches to characterizing neural systems, from molecular and synaptic processes to cognition and behavior.

name: Tai Sing Lee
title: Professor
department: Neuroscience Institute, School of Computer Science
school: School of Computer Science and Neuroscience Institute
bio: Tai Sing Lee is a Professor who studies the characterization of neural circuits and computational neuroscience to understand how the brain processes information. His research employs computational, mathematical, and statistical methods along with physiological and anatomical approaches to investigate learning, memory, sensation, and perception.

name: Brad Mahon
title: Associate Professor
bio: Brad Mahon is an Associate Professor in the Neuroscience Institute and Psychology department who studies the cognitive neuroscience underlying human thought and behavior. His research uses brain imaging and other neuroscientific methods to understand neural mechanisms of cognition.

name: Tom Mitchell
title: Founders University Professor
department: School of Computer Science, Language Technologies Institute, Robotics Institute
bio: Tom Mitchell is a Founders University Professor working at the intersection of machine learning, computational biology, and neuroscience. His research focuses on developing machine learning methods and applying them to understand biological systems and human cognition.

name: Linda Moya
title: Distinguished Service Professor
bio: Linda Moya studies the neuroscience of decision making, focusing on how the social and emotional brain influence decision processes using neuroimaging methods including MEG, EEG, and structural MRI. Her research integrates systems, behavioral, decision, and cognitive neuroscience approaches to understand visual attention and cognitive processing in the brain.

name: Abby Noyce
title: Research Professor
bio: Abby Noyce is a Research Professor at the Neuroscience Institute whose work spans auditory research, cognitive neuroscience, and behavioral methods. Her research focuses on understanding executive control, memory, attention, and perception through non-invasive brain monitoring and spatial cognition studies.

name: Burak Ozdoganlar
title: Professor
bio: Burak Ozdoganlar is a Professor of Mechanical Engineering whose research bridges mechanical engineering and neuroscience. His work focuses on precision systems and manufacturing at micro-scales with applications to biomedical and neuroscience research.

name: Rahul Panat
title: Associate Professor
department: Mechanical Engineering and Civil and Environmental Engineering

name: Andreas Pfenning
title: Assistant Professor
bio: Andreas Pfenning is an Assistant Professor studying computational approaches to understanding neural systems and brain function. His research combines computational, mathematical, and statistical methods to investigate motor control, language processing, and neurological diseases.

name: Xaq Pitkow
title: Associate Professor
bio: Xaq Pitkow studies how the brain computes with neurons, using tools from neuroscience, mathematics, and machine learning to understand neural representations and information processing. His research spans neural coding, visual perception, decision-making, and the development of methods to decode and model brain function.

name: David Plaut
title: Professor
bio: David Plaut is a Professor of Psychology and core faculty member of the Neuroscience Institute who uses computational and mathematical methods to investigate cognitive neuroscience. His research spans language processing, reading, developmental processes, and neural mechanisms underlying perception and behavior.

name: Cosma Shalizi
title: Associate Professor
bio: Cosma Shalizi is an Associate Professor of Statistics and Machine Learning who develops statistical and computational methods for understanding complex systems and extracting knowledge from data. His research spans machine learning, causal inference, and the analysis of networked and dependent data.

name: Barbara Shinn-Cunningham
title: George A. and Helen Dunham Cowan Professor of Auditory Neuroscience; Director, Neuroscience Institute
bio: Barbara Shinn-Cunningham's research combines behavioral, neuroimaging, and computational methods to understand how the brain processes sound, with particular expertise in spatial hearing, auditory attention, and sensory deficits. She directs the Neuroscience Institute at Carnegie Mellon and holds courtesy appointments across multiple departments including Psychology, Biomedical Engineering, and Electrical and Computer Engineering.

name: Aarti Singh
title: Associate Professor
bio: Aarti Singh is an Associate Professor of Machine Learning who develops statistical and computational methods for high-dimensional data analysis and inference. Her research spans machine learning, optimization, and neuroscience applications.

name: Matthew Smith
title: Professor
bio: Matthew Smith is a Professor in Biomedical Engineering and core faculty member of the Neuroscience Institute, serving as Co-Director of the Center for the Neural Basis of Cognition. His research focuses on characterizing neural circuits underlying motor control, spatial cognition, and perception using computational, physiological, and behavioral approaches.

name: Mike Tarr
title: Kavcic-Moura Professor of Cognitive and Brain Science & Department Head
bio: Mike Tarr is a Kavcic-Moura Professor of Cognitive and Brain Science and Department Head who studies how the brain perceives and processes visual information using computational and behavioral methods. His research combines cognitive neuroscience approaches with non-invasive brain monitoring techniques to understand the neural mechanisms underlying perception and cognition.

name: Dave Touretzky
title: Research Professor
bio: Dave Touretzky is a Research Professor studying computational neuroscience, with expertise in learning, memory, and spatial cognition. His work integrates computational, mathematical, and statistical methods to understand neural mechanisms underlying behavior and cognition.

name: Jonathan Tsay
title: Assistant Professor
bio: Jonathan Tsay is an assistant professor studying fundamental processes in sensorimotor learning, including how the brain adapts motor behavior through implicit and explicit mechanisms. His research combines behavioral experiments, computational modeling, and large-scale citizen science to understand proprioceptive re-alignment and motor skill acquisition.

name: Valerie Ventura
title: Associate Professor
bio: Valerie Ventura is an Associate Professor of Statistics at the Neuroscience Institute who develops statistical and computational methods for analyzing neural data. Her research focuses on Bayesian inference and probabilistic modeling approaches to understand brain function and neural circuits.

name: Timothy Verstynen
title: Associate Professor
bio: Timothy Verstynen is an Associate Professor and Interim Director of the Neuroscience Institute who studies neural circuits underlying cognition, motor control, and learning using computational and non-invasive brain monitoring methods. His research integrates behavioral methods, mathematical modeling, and neuroimaging to understand how the brain solves complex cognitive and motor problems.

name: Vibha Viswanathan
title: Visiting Assistant Professor
bio: Vibha Viswanathan studies the neural mechanisms of auditory scene analysis and speech intelligibility in complex listening environments using electroencephalography, psychophysics, and computational modeling. She leverages these findings to improve listening outcomes through auditory training and brain-computer interface applications.

name: Saurabh Vyas
title: Assistant Professor
bio: Saurabh Vyas is an Assistant Professor at CMU's Neuroscience Institute who leads the Laboratory of General Intelligence and Computation (LOGIC), focusing on understanding neural mechanisms underlying cognition, learning, and decision-making. His research integrates computational, mathematical, and statistical methods to characterize neural circuits involved in behavioral control, memory, and reasoning.

name: Doug Weber
title: Associate Professor
bio: Doug Weber is an Associate Professor in Mechanical Engineering and core faculty member of the Neuroscience Institute who specializes in computational neuroscience and neural engineering. His research focuses on motor control, sensation and perception, and the development of non-invasive brain monitoring technologies.

name: Leila Wehbe
title: Associate Professor
bio: Leila Wehbe is an Associate Professor in the Machine Learning Department and core faculty member of the Neuroscience Institute who combines computational methods with neuroscience to understand how the brain processes language and sensory information. Her research develops novel statistical and mathematical approaches for analyzing non-invasive brain monitoring data to illuminate cognitive processes.

name: Sossena Wood
title: Assistant Professor
bio: Sossena Wood is an Assistant Professor in Biomedical Engineering and core faculty member of the Neuroscience Institute who studies cognitive neuroscience using advanced neural engineering and monitoring techniques. Her research focuses on understanding learning, memory, and neurological diseases through behavioral methods and non-invasive brain monitoring approaches.

name: Byron Yu
title: Gerard G. Elia Career Development Professor
department: Electrical & Computer Engineering, Biomedical Engineering, Neuroscience Institute
bio: Byron Yu is a computational neuroscientist who develops mathematical and statistical methods to understand how neural circuits control behavior and process information. His research combines behavioral experiments, neural recordings, and computational modeling to investigate motor control, learning, and sensory processing.

name: Bruce Armitage
title: Professor, Department Head
bio: Bruce Armitage designs and synthesizes peptide nucleic acid oligomers for biological imaging, sensing, and probing of DNA and RNA sequences. His research focuses on fluorescent labeling, DNA nanotechnology, and targeting G quadruplexes for understanding gene regulation and treating genetic and infectious diseases.

name: Stefan Bernhard
title: Professor
bio: Stefan Bernhard's lab investigates light-to-electrochemical energy conversions using transition metal complexes as photosensitizers and catalysts for renewable energy applications including water photolysis and CO₂-to-fuel conversions. His research combines traditional synthetic chemistry with modern automation techniques using 3D printing and machine vision to conduct high-throughput screening of photocatalytic materials and explore structure-property relationships in chromophores.

name: Mark E. Bier
title: Research Professor
bio: Mark E. Bier directs the Center for Molecular Analysis, which provides modern high-performance analytical instruments and expertise to researchers at CMU and external institutions. He specializes in advanced ultra-high m/z heavy ion mass spectrometry analysis and offers collaborative analytical assistance and training.

name: Mark Bier
title: Research Professor
bio: Mark Bier develops advanced mass spectrometry instrumentation for analyzing heavy ions and nanoparticles, including biomacromolecular complexes like viruses and synthetic nanoparticles. His research focuses on improving inlet technologies, ionization sources, and detectors, with particular expertise in superconducting tunnel junction cryodetection and applications ranging from environmental water analysis to DNA sequencing.

name: Emile Bominaar
title: Associate Research Professor
bio: Emile Bominaar studies the electronic structure of transition metal complexes and metal centers in biology, with a focus on understanding how molecular geometry relates to physical properties and reactivity. His research employs computational methods such as density functional theory combined with experimental spectroscopic techniques including Mössbauer spectroscopy and magneto-optical measurements.

name: Terrence J. Collins
title: Teresa Heinz Professor in Green Chemistry
bio: Terrence Collins develops green oxidation catalysts that mimic natural peroxidase enzymes to activate hydrogen peroxide and oxygen for water purification and pollutant degradation. His research focuses on designing high-performance catalysts at low concentrations that can oxidize persistent contaminants including pharmaceuticals, dyes, pesticides, and chemical warfare agents in water.

name: Subha R. Das
title: Associate Professor
bio: Subha Das develops novel chemical methods for engineering nucleic acid structures and polymers, with applications in nanobiotechnology and biomaterials. Her research includes DNA nanostructures with controlled geometry, RNA modifications for exosome engineering, and hybrid DNA-polymer materials with enhanced functionality.

name: Neil M. Donahue
title: Thomas Lord University Professor of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
bio: Neil Donahue studies the oxidation chemistry of Earth's atmosphere, with a focus on organic compounds, radical processes, and the formation of organic aerosols that impact climate and human health. His research explores the dynamic balance between phase partitioning, oxidation chemistry, and the fate of short-lived intermediates in gas-phase ozonolysis, using both experimental and theoretical approaches.

name: Isaac Garcia Bosch
title: Associate Professor
bio: Garcia-Bosch develops metal complexes inspired by metalloenzymes to catalyze organic transformations under environmentally benign conditions using first-row transition metals and green oxidants. His research focuses on copper-based catalysts for practical synthetic applications including C-H hydroxylation, alcohol oxidation, and dioxygen reduction.

name: Roberto R. Gil
title: Research Professor
bio: Roberto Gil is Director of the NMR Center and specializes in developing advanced NMR methodologies for analyzing small molecules, natural products, and biological macromolecules using anisotropic media and residual coupling parameters. His research focuses on structure elucidation of bioactive natural products and the development of innovative polymer gels as alignment media for high-resolution NMR analysis.

name: Gabriel Gomes
title: Assistant Professor
bio: Gabriel Gomes develops computational and experimental approaches to accelerate catalyst discovery and chemical reaction design using artificial intelligence and machine learning. His research combines physical organic chemistry principles with data-driven strategies to understand and optimize homogeneous catalysis and automated synthesis.

name: Yisong (Alex) Guo
title: Professor
bio: Yisong Guo studies iron-containing metalloproteins that catalyze essential biological processes like nitrogen fixation, photosynthesis, and respiration using advanced spectroscopic techniques including Mössbauer and EPR spectroscopy. His research aims to understand the electronic and geometric structures of metal centers in enzymes to reveal fundamental chemical principles and inform the design of catalysts for important reactions like water oxidation and dinitrogen reduction.

name: Michael P. Hendrich
title: Professor
bio: Michael Hendrich studies metalloproteins and metalloenzymes at the atomic level to understand their catalytic function, with a focus on essential biological processes including DNA synthesis, metabolism, and photosynthesis. His research employs advanced spectroscopic techniques including EPR, ENDOR, Mössbauer spectroscopy, and SQUID magnetization to characterize reaction intermediates and probe metal centers in proteins and biomimetic complexes.

name: Olexandr Isayev
title: Carl and Amy Jones Professor in Interdisciplinary Science
bio: Olexandr Isayev's lab works at the interface of theoretical chemistry, pharmaceutical sciences, and computer science, using molecular simulations and artificial intelligence to solve difficult chemistry problems. His research has been groundbreaking in machine learning and quantum mechanics, developing atomistic molecular ML potentials and generative AI methods for inverse design of molecules with desired properties.

name: Rongchao Jin
title: Professor
bio: Rongchao Jin's group focuses on atomically precise nanochemistry, developing synthetic principles for creating nanoparticles with perfect atomic-level structures and characterized compositions. His research has revealed fundamental principles of nanoparticle assembly, growth periodicities, and emergent phenomena with applications in catalysis, energy conversion, optoelectronics, and sensing.

name: Anna M. Kietrys
title: Assistant Professor
bio: Anna M. Kietrys applies chemical, biochemical, and bioinformatics tools to understand RNA-mediated cell signaling in neurodegeneration, focusing on the dynamics of RNA structure and its role as an information carrier in disease progression. Her group leverages advanced techniques like 2′-OH acylation to develop reagents that elucidate the RNA interactome and explore therapeutic approaches including RNA-based treatments for Parkinson's disease.

name: Hyung J. Kim
title: Professor
bio: Hyung J. Kim is a theoretical and computational chemist who studies solvation effects on chemical reactions and dynamics in environmentally benign green solvents using analytic theory and modern computational techniques. His research also encompasses the computational study of supercapacitors, pseudocapacitors, and multi-domain protein conformational changes.

name: Tomasz Kowalewski
title: Professor
bio: Kowalewski's research focuses on self-organization of macromolecules to create novel nanostructured materials, spanning molecular design through device characterization. His work emphasizes the development of atomic force microscopy techniques and exploration of applications in energy storage, organic electronics, and polymer systems.

name: Maria Kurnikova
title: Professor
bio: Maria Kurnikova's research focuses on computational chemistry and theoretical biophysics to understand structure-function relationships in proteins, particularly membrane-associated proteins and ion channels. Her group uses high-performance computing and machine learning methods to predict biological macromolecules' behavior and energetics, bridging atomistic-level understanding with experimental observations.

name: Danith Ly
title: Professor
bio: Professor Ly leads the Institute for Biomolecular Design and Discovery, focusing on the design of molecular tools for regulating gene expression and probing protein dynamics in human stem cells. His research spans protein design, tissue engineering, and molecular self-assembly, with expertise in natural and synthetic biopolymer design including DNA, RNA, and proteins.

name: Krzysztof Matyjaszewski
title: J. C. Warner University Professor of Natural Sciences, Chemistry
bio: Krzysztof Matyjaszewski is a leading researcher in macromolecular engineering who develops precisely controlled polymers through living and controlled polymerization techniques, particularly atom transfer radical polymerization (ATRP). His work encompasses the synthesis of well-defined macromolecules with applications in optoelectronic, biomedical, and energy storage systems, including the preparation of organic/inorganic hybrids and catalytic materials.

name: Carrie McDonough
title: Associate Professor
bio: Carrie McDonough investigates organic pollutants, their transformation and transport through the environment, and their impacts on water quality and human health using advanced analytical strategies like high-resolution mass spectrometry and ion mobility. Her work emphasizes identifying and prioritizing synthetic organics based on bioavailability and bioaccumulation, and developing materials and workflows to assess environmental and public health risks.

name: Kevin Noonan
title: Professor
bio: Kevin Noonan's research focuses on the synthesis of new compounds with main group elements to impact polymer chemistry and catalysis, including the incorporation of boron and phosphorus into electronic materials. His group also develops sophisticated ligand frameworks using organophosphorus chemistry for catalyst systems in polyolefin synthesis and functional organic substrate preparation.

name: Linda Peteanu
title: Professor
bio: Linda Peteanu uses fluorescence microscopy-based methods to investigate electronic and optical properties of conjugated polymers, nanomaterials, and their applications in molecular electronics and quantum technologies. Her research focuses on understanding energy and charge transport in conjugated materials, plasmonic effects on fluorescence, and optimization of these materials for lighting, displays, and photovoltaic devices.

name: Gizelle Sherwood
title: Teaching Professor, Director of Undergraduate Studies & Laboratories
bio: Gizelle Sherwood is a Teaching Professor focused on foundational chemistry education for freshman and sophomore students, incorporating both traditional and technology-enhanced teaching methods. She is actively involved in developing K-12 chemistry outreach programs through partnerships with community organizations including the Gelfand Center, Pittsburgh Junior Academy of Science, and the Boy Scouts of America.

name: Gloria Silva
title: Teaching Professor
bio: Gloria Silva specializes in bioorganic chemistry with expertise in fluorescent dyes synthesis, peptides and peptide nucleic acids synthesis, and molecular recognition of DNA and G-quadruplexes. She is also engaged in natural product chemistry research focused on bioactive natural products, separation and purification methods, and spectroscopic analysis.

name: Ryan Sullivan
title: Professor
bio: Ryan Sullivan's research focuses on understanding the sources and chemical transformations of environmental contaminants, including their persistence, transformation products, and impacts on humans and the environment. His work combines atmospheric aerosol chemistry, sustainable chemistry approaches, and advanced analytical techniques to study environmental molecular lifecycles and develop methods for pollutant remediation.

name: Stefanie A. Sydlik
title: Associate Professor
bio: Prof. Sydlik develops chemical methods and materials to improve human health, focusing on structure-function relationships in materials at the border of chemistry and engineering. Her research leverages organic chemistry to synthesize smart biomaterials that can program stem cell fate and scaffold properties, with platforms including functional graphenic materials and polymers for applications in tissue regeneration, controlled drug release, and environmental remediation.

name: Stefanie Sydlik
title: Professor
bio: Stefanie Sydlik develops chemical methods and smart biomaterials to improve human health, focusing on structure-function relationships at the interface of chemistry and engineering. Her research uses functional graphenic materials and polymers with rational design principles to program stem cell fate and create bioactive scaffolds for applications including bone regeneration, drug overdose treatment, and heavy metal remediation.

name: Leonard Vuocolo
title: Associate Teaching Professor
bio: Leonard Vuocolo is an Associate Teaching Professor focused on innovative approaches to chemistry education, emphasizing concepts-first learning and technology integration at the introductory level. His research interests include green chemistry implementation, bioinorganic chemistry in the curriculum, interdisciplinary experiments, and evidence-based assessment methods such as specifications grading.

name: Newell R. Washburn
title: Associate Professor
bio: Newell Washburn's research focuses on the intersection of materials chemistry and sustainability, developing fundamental understanding of widely-used materials to reduce their environmental impact. His group employs machine learning as a central tool for designing biobased polyurethane elastomers and limestone/calcined clay cements, with students gaining expertise in both laboratory research and data science.

name: David Yaron
title: Professor
bio: David Yaron develops machine learning approaches to reduce the computational cost of quantum chemistry while maintaining high accuracy through semiempirical quantum chemical models. He also creates learning engineering resources for chemistry education at the college and high school levels, utilizing student interaction data to improve educational materials.

name: Edwin N. Lassettre
title: Professor
bio: Edwin N. Lassettre was a Carnegie Mellon University faculty member internationally known for pioneering work in electron-impact spectroscopy. He made significant contributions to the field of physical chemistry during his exemplary career.

name: John Alison
title: Associate Professor
bio: John Alison studies Higgs boson pair production and its self-interaction properties to probe physics beyond the Standard Model and understand fundamental particle mass acquisition. His research combines experimental particle physics with machine learning techniques to extract physics insights from Large Hadron Collider data and detector development for the CMS experiment.

name: David Anderson
title: Associate Teaching Professor
bio: David Anderson is an Associate Teaching Professor focused on developing experimental physics laboratory courses and connecting fundamental physics skills to life-science applications. His research interests span physics education research, semiconductor heterostructures, and effective pedagogical methodologies for teaching physics to diverse student populations.

name: Shelley L. Anna
title: Professor
bio: Shelley Anna is a professor of chemical engineering whose research focuses on multiphase microfluidics, interfacial rheology, and microscale transport phenomena. She is a fellow of the American Physical Society and has received numerous awards including the NSF CAREER award and the George Tallman Ladd Research Award.

name: Axel Brandenburg
title: Adjunct Professor
bio: Axel Brandenburg is a leading researcher in astrophysical fluid dynamics and magnetohydrodynamics, with expertise in magnetic field generation from turbulent motions across the Sun, stars, accretion disks, galaxies, and the early Universe. His pioneering work includes the first compressible dynamo simulations, fundamental discoveries about magneto-rotational instabilities, inverse cascades of magnetic fields, and innovative methods for measuring magnetic helicity in solar systems.

name: Katelyn Breivik
title: Assistant Professor
bio: Katelyn Breivik studies binary star interactions and their role in stellar population evolution by combining theoretical simulations, electromagnetic surveys, and gravitational wave observations. She develops public software tools for binary population synthesis and is committed to reproducible science in understanding how binary stars shape outcomes across astrophysics and cosmology.

name: Roy A. Briere
title: Professor of Physics
bio: Roy Briere is a high-energy particle physicist specializing in flavor physics, the study of quark decays and properties, with particular focus on CP violation and rare decays of B and D mesons. He leads research efforts on the BelleII experiment in Japan and has been instrumental in the BESIII experiment in Beijing, contributing to measurements of decay constants, form factors, and branching fractions critical to understanding weak interactions.

name: Shubhayu Chatterjee
title: Assistant Professor
bio: Shubhayu Chatterjee is an Assistant Professor of Physics studying quantum many-body physics in strongly correlated electronic systems, with applications to quantum simulators and quantum sensing. His research explores topological phases, superconductivity, and the use of quantum sensors to probe exotic material properties.

name: Juan Chamorro
title: Assistant Professor
bio: Juan Chamorro is an assistant professor researching quantum materials synthesis, crystal growth, and characterization using X-ray and neutron scattering techniques. His work integrates materials science, inorganic chemistry, and condensed matter physics to understand the properties of quantum materials.

name: Matteo Cremonesi
title: Assistant Professor
bio: Matteo Cremonesi is an experimental particle physicist studying fundamental building blocks of nature, with a focus on dark matter searches at the Large Hadron Collider using the CMS experiment. His research integrates machine learning techniques, particularly graph neural networks, to improve missing transverse momentum reconstruction for collider physics analysis and real-time event triggering on custom electronics.

name: Rupert A. C. Croft
title: Professor of Physics
bio: Rupert Croft studies the formation of large-scale structures in the Universe using numerical simulations and observational data from telescopes and surveys. His research focuses on understanding how gravity amplifies tiny perturbations to form galaxies and galaxy clusters, and on constraining theories of cosmic evolution.

name: Markus Deserno
title: Professor of Physics
bio: Markus Deserno is a theoretical and computational biophysicist who studies biological systems by applying well-established physical laws to understand complex phenomena at mesoscopic scales. His research focuses on lipid membranes, proteins, viruses, and DNA, using coarse-grained simulations and tools from continuum elasticity and statistical mechanics to reveal fundamental physical principles governing biological behavior.

name: Tiziana Di Matteo
title: Professor of Physics
bio: Tiziana Di Matteo studies black holes across cosmic history, focusing on the interplay between black hole growth and galaxy formation through theoretical and computational approaches. Her research encompasses cosmological simulations, accretion physics, and observational studies using X-ray and radio data to understand how supermassive black holes shape galaxy evolution.

name: Scott Dodelson
title: Professor of Physics
bio: Scott Dodelson studies fundamental physics through analysis of data from cosmic surveys, investigating dark matter, dark energy, and inflation. He serves as co-chair of the Science Committee for the Dark Energy Survey and is actively involved in LSST and South Pole Telescope observations.

name: Valentina Dutta
title: Assistant Professor
bio: Valentina Dutta's research focuses on searching for new physics beyond the Standard Model, particularly through studies of supersymmetry and dark matter using data from the Large Hadron Collider's CMS experiment. She leverages machine learning techniques to improve sensitivity in discovering new phenomena that could explain mysteries like dark matter and the light mass of the Higgs boson.

name: Frank Heinrich
title: Associate Research Professor
bio: Frank Heinrich studies the structure of disease-relevant proteins, peptides, and small molecules at lipid membranes, with a focus on understanding the structural foundations of cell signaling in cancer. He employs surface-sensitive techniques including electrical impedance spectroscopy, surface plasmon resonance, and neutron reflectometry, and is actively involved in developing next-generation neutron scattering instrumentation for biological and soft-matter research.

name: Shirley Ho
title: Adjunct Professor
bio: Shirley Ho investigates the dark components of the Universe and its origins using the spatial distribution of galaxies, quasars, and neutral hydrogen as standard rulers, particularly through baryon acoustic oscillations and cosmic microwave background measurements. She combines observations from surveys like SDSS and Planck with large-scale computational analysis to constrain dark energy models and understand the matter content of the Universe.

name: Benjamin M. Hunt
title: Associate Professor
bio: Benjamin Hunt is an Associate Professor of Physics at Carnegie Mellon University studying condensed matter physics, particularly electron behavior under extreme conditions such as ultra-low temperatures and high magnetic fields. His research focuses on van der Waals heterostructures of two-dimensional crystals and their mesoscopic device properties, including phenomena like superconductivity and charge fractionalization.

name: Mohammad F. Islam
title: Professor
department: Department of Materials Science and Engineering
bio: Mohammad Islam is a professor studying advanced materials processing, manufacturing, and sustainable energy applications, with particular expertise in carbon nanotubes and soft/bioactive materials. His research spans additive manufacturing, nanostructures, and energy technologies, with applications in EVs, direct air capture, and medical devices.

name: Tina Kahniashvili
title: Research Professor
bio: Tina Kahniashvili studies physical processes in the early and late universe, including gravitational wave signals from inflation and phase transitions, primordial magnetic field evolution, and tests of fundamental symmetries at high energies. Her research connects observational cosmology with high energy particle physics to understand the origin, structure, and evolution of the universe.

name: Jyoti Katoch
title: Associate Professor
bio: Jyoti Katoch investigates the electronic, optical, and spin-dependent properties of novel quantum materials, particularly two-dimensional materials and their devices, with expertise in controlling material properties through atomic-scale modifications. Her research combines the growth of quantum materials using molecular beam epitaxy with state-of-the-art characterization techniques including in-situ magneto-transport measurements and nanoscale angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy.

name: Vladyslav Kozii
title: Assistant Professor
bio: Vladyslav Kozii studies exotic superconductivity and strongly interacting topological systems in condensed matter physics, with particular focus on the microscopic origins of topological superconductors and transport properties of topological materials. His research addresses fundamental questions while maintaining connections to experimental findings in topics ranging from quantum critical points to twisted bilayer graphene.

name: Rachel Kurchin
title: Assistant Research Professor
bio: Rachel Kurchin is a computational materials scientist who uses electronic structure theory, data science, and energy device modeling to address climate challenges. She develops scientific codes, leads the ACME research group, and conducts research on materials computation, renewable energy, and electrochemical synthesis.

name: Sara A. Majetich
title: Professor of Physics
bio: Sara Majetich's research focuses on uniform magnetic nanoparticles and their fundamental behavior, including self-assembly into ordered arrays and collective phenomena analogous to atomic crystals. Her work explores applications in data storage, permanent magnets, and biomedicine, as well as the development of nanocomposites using inorganic matrix coatings.

name: Sayan Mandal
title: Special Lecturer
bio: Sayan Mandal is a theoretical cosmologist whose research focuses on dark matter physics, gravitational wave production in the early universe, and primordial magnetic field evolution. His work combines observational cosmology with theoretical modeling to understand fundamental physics from inflation through the present day.

name: Rachel Mandelbaum
title: Professor and Department Head
bio: Rachel Mandelbaum's research focuses on observational cosmology and galaxy studies using weak gravitational lensing and advanced data analysis techniques. She leads major cosmological surveys including Hyper-SuprimeCam and Rubin Observatory LSST to constrain dark energy and understand galaxy properties.

name: Noa Marom
title: Associate Professor
bio: Noa Marom is an Associate Professor who uses computational methods and machine learning to discover and design new materials for energy applications and semiconductors. Her research combines quantum mechanical simulations, optimization algorithms, and large-scale computing to address challenges in materials science and energy security.

name: Michael E. McHenry
title: Professor
bio: Michael McHenry studies soft magnetic nano-composites and their structural and magnetic properties for power and energy applications, with expertise in field processing and high-temperature magnetic materials. His research spans advanced materials processing, aerospace engineering, and sustainable energy production, with applications to motors, power electronics, and grid modernization.

name: Curtis A. Meyer
title: Professor
bio: Curtis Meyer studies the fundamental structure of matter by investigating quark confinement and the behavior of quarks inside nucleons through quantum chromodynamics. He leads the GlueX experiment at Jefferson Lab, which experimentally maps the spectrum of exotic hybrid mesons predicted by lattice QCD calculations.

name: Colin Morningstar
title: Professor of Physics
bio: Colin Morningstar studies nonperturbative phenomena in quantum field theories, with emphasis on hadron formation and confinement in quantum chromodynamics using computer simulations of quarks and gluons. His research focuses on ab initio determinations of excited baryon and meson spectra using lattice QCD Monte Carlo methods on supercomputers.

name: Brendan Mullan
title: Associate Teaching Professor
bio: Brendan Mullan is an associate teaching professor and Director of Undergraduate Laboratories who guides the evolution of experimental physics courses using data-informed practices and physics education research. He is an accomplished science communicator and explorer with expertise in astronomy, astrobiology, and inquiry-based teaching methodologies.

name: Antonella Palmese
title: Assistant Professor
bio: Antonella Palmese is an observational cosmologist and astronomer who studies the universe's content and evolution using galaxy surveys and gravitational wave data to understand cosmic dynamics and the origins of gravitational wave sources. Her research integrates large galaxy surveys, time domain astronomy, and gravitational wave experiments, with focus on multimessenger astronomy and standard siren cosmology.

name: Diana Parno
title: Falco-DeBenedetti Associate Professor of Physics
bio: Diana Parno studies the physics of neutrinos, fundamental particles that interact only via the weak force, with a focus on measuring their absolute mass scale and low-energy interactions with matter. Her research uses experiments like KATRIN and COHERENT to test the edges of the Standard Model while providing insights into cosmic structure formation and physics beyond electroweak theory.

name: Manfred Paulini
title: Professor of Physics
bio: Manfred Paulini is an experimental high energy physicist studying the interface between particle physics and cosmology, with focus on matter-antimatter asymmetry and dark matter. He recently expanded his research to apply machine learning and deep learning techniques for event classification in collider data analysis.

name: Riccardo Penco
title: Associate Professor
bio: Riccardo Penco uses quantum field theory techniques to draw connections between cosmology, astrophysics, and condensed matter systems, ranging from inflation and dark matter to tidal dynamics and superradiance. His research emphasizes the dynamics of collective excitations, symmetry realization at finite density, and the development of effective field theory approaches to gravitational and cosmological phenomena.

name: Jeffrey B. Peterson
title: Professor of Physics
bio: Peterson's research group designs and builds novel radio telescopes at remote sites to study dark energy, fast radio bursts, and the formation of the first stars after the Big Bang. His team has developed advanced telescope designs including cylindrical reflectors, satellite dish arrays, and precision spectrometers deployed at locations worldwide.

name: Anthony Rollett
title: University Professor
bio: Anthony Rollett's research focuses on microstructural evolution and microstructure-property relationships in 3D using both experiments and simulations, with particular emphasis on 3D printing of metals and materials for energy conversion systems. He is the PI and co-director of the Institute for Model-Based Qualification & Certification of Additive Manufacturing (IMQCAM), a NASA-supported research institute, and co-director of the Next Manufacturing Center.

name: Rachel Rosen
title: Associate Professor
bio: Rachel Rosen's research focuses on the intersection of quantum field theory and gravity, with emphasis on improving our understanding of gravity using particle physics methodology and theoretical consistency conditions for quantum field theories. Her work includes applications of effective field theory techniques to various states of matter, from ordinary fluids to quantum liquids in dense stellar cores.

name: Ira Z. Rothstein
title: Professor of Physics
bio: Ira Rothstein's research focuses on the application of effective field theory to study complex, non-linear, strongly coupled systems across multiple scales in physics. His work spans Higgs boson production at the LHC, strong interaction dynamics, gravitational wave profiles from inspiralling black holes measured at LIGO, and quantum aspects of solids.

name: Gillian Lynn Ryan
title: Associate Teaching Professor
bio: Gillian Lynn Ryan is the Director of Undergraduate Affairs and Associate Teaching Professor in the Department of Physics, focusing on supporting undergraduate student development and success. Her research interests include cell protrusion dynamics, actin polymerization, and computational modeling of cell movement.

name: Sufei Shi
title: Associate Professor
bio: Sufei Shi's research integrates nanomaterial synthesis and nanoscale fabrication with electrical and optical measurements to study electron-photon interactions in low-dimensional systems. His work focuses on designing and characterizing novel nanoelectronic and optoelectronic devices using two-dimensional materials and van der Waals heterostructures.

name: Fangwei Si
title: Cooper-Siegel Assistant Professor of Physics
bio: Fangwei Si develops experimental tools and quantitative approaches to discover fundamental biological laws underlying living systems. His research focuses on bacterial cell surfaces, cellular adaptation mechanisms, and bacteria-phage interactions using single-cell microfluidics and imaging.

name: Simranjeet Singh
title: Associate Professor
bio: Simranjeet Singh studies spins and magnetism phenomena in emergent quantum materials using atomically precise engineering and advanced experimental techniques. His research focuses on exploiting topology and symmetry breaking for device applications, spin dynamics in quantum materials, and developing quantum sensing platforms.

name: Marek Skowronski
title: Professor
department: Department of Materials Science and Engineering
bio: Professor Skowronski discovers, understands, and fabricates novel materials, phenomena, and device structures for information technology with a focus on increasing energy efficiency for artificial intelligence applications. His research includes artificial synapses for neuromorphic computing, non-volatile memory and logic devices, and beyond-Moore electronics.

name: Grigory Tarnopolskiy
title: Assistant Professor
bio: Grigory Tarnopolskiy researches problems in high energy physics theory and condensed matter physics theory, with a focus on twistronics and moiré superlattices in twisted bilayer graphene. His recent work also examines critical phenomena including the Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev model and systems exhibiting non-Fermi liquid behavior and spontaneous symmetry breaking.

name: Hy Trac
title: Associate Professor
bio: Hy Trac develops and applies numerical simulations to model cosmology and astrophysics, studying gas, galaxies, quasars, and clusters to understand dark matter and dark energy. His research also encompasses stellar dynamics, compact object mergers producing gravitational waves, and the application of AI/ML methods to multi-wavelength data analysis.

name: Diane Turnshek
title: Special Lecturer
bio: Diane Turnshek is a Special Lecturer in Physics with expertise in astronomy and spectroscopy, including the study of cool stars and celestial objects. Her research interests include light pollution and skyglow, applying remote sensing techniques to understand the impact of artificial lighting on the night sky.

name: Matthew G. Walker
title: Professor
bio: Matthew Walker studies the astrophysical properties of dark matter through optical imaging, spectroscopy, and dynamical modeling of dwarf galaxies that surround the Milky Way and Andromeda. His research aims to measure the spatial distribution of dark matter at small scales to help determine the fundamental nature of dark matter particles.

name: Michael Widom
title: Professor of Physics
bio: Michael Widom's research focuses on theoretical modeling of novel materials in condensed matter and biological physics using statistical mechanics, quantum mechanics, and computer simulation. His major research areas include noncrystalline metals, metallic glasses, quasicrystals, and high-entropy alloys studied through first-principles calculations and statistical ensemble modeling.

name: Jian-Gang (Jimmy) Zhu
title: Professor
bio: Jimmy Zhu is the ABB Professor of Electrical and Computer Engineering and director of the Data Storage Systems Center, a world-leading research institution in hard drive technology. His research focuses on magnetic recording, data storage systems, spintronics, and the development of advanced materials and modeling techniques for next-generation computing and storage applications.

name: Ignacio Arana Araya
title: Assistant Professor
department: Carnegie Mellon Institute for Strategy & Technology
school: Carnegie Mellon Institute for Strategy and Technology
bio: Dr. Arana is a comparativist who specializes in how personality traits of heads of government impact executive governance and examines the consequences of variation in political institutions across countries, with emphasis on Latin America. His research focuses on executive-legislative relations, informal institutions, gender and politics, and judicial politics.

name: Hannah Bailey
title: Assistant Professor
department: Carnegie Mellon Institute for Strategy and Technology
school: Carnegie Mellon Institute for Strategy and Technology
bio: Hannah Bailey is an Assistant Professor at CMIST whose research examines how authoritarian states develop and use emerging technologies, with a focus on state-sponsored digital persuasion in the global public sphere. She develops and applies natural language processing, machine learning, and computer vision techniques to analyze large-scale text and image datasets.

name: Haleigh Bartos
title: Associate Professor of the Practice
department: Carnegie Mellon Institute for Strategy & Technology
school: Carnegie Mellon Institute for Strategy and Technology
bio: Haleigh Bartos is an Associate Professor of the Practice at CMIST with over a decade of experience in Washington, DC supporting policy work and NGO initiatives. Her research focuses on sub-Saharan Africa, national security, and terrorism, with publications in the Journal of International Women's Studies.

name: Justin Key Canfil
title: Assistant Professor
department: Carnegie Mellon Institute for Strategy & Technology
school: Carnegie Mellon Institute for Strategy and Technology
bio: Justin Key Canfil is a political scientist researching international security, international law, innovation theory, and science and technology competition. His book project examines how arms controllers engineer against the future and the consequences of their failures.

name: Jonathan Cervas
title: Assistant Teaching Professor
department: Carnegie Mellon Institute for Strategy & Technology
school: Carnegie Mellon Institute for Strategy and Technology
bio: Jonathan Cervas is an Assistant Teaching Professor specializing in redistricting, voting rights, and American political institutions. He has extensive experience as a redistricting consultant and expert witness in federal court cases, and has published peer-reviewed research on elections and political representation.

name: John Chin
title: Assistant Teaching Professor
department: Carnegie Mellon Institute for Strategy & Technology
school: Carnegie Mellon Institute for Strategy and Technology
bio: John Chin is an Assistant Teaching Professor whose research focuses on technologies of rebellion including coups, mass protest, and nonviolent revolution, as well as authoritarian politics and comparative democratization. He is the lead author of the Historical Dictionary of Modern Coups D'état and has published extensively in peer-reviewed political science journals on political violence and instability.

name: Mieke Eoyang
title: Visiting Professor
department: Carnegie Mellon Institute for Strategy & Technology
school: Carnegie Mellon Institute for Strategy and Technology
bio: Mieke Eoyang is a national security policy expert who previously served as Deputy Assistant Secretary of Defense for Cyber Policy, where she was responsible for cybersecurity and electronic warfare strategy at the Department of Defense. She has extensive experience as a think tank leader and Congressional advisor developing policies on cybercrime, ransomware, surveillance, and privacy.

name: Baruch Fischhoff
title: Howard Heinz University Professor
department: Department of Engineering and Public Policy
school: Carnegie Mellon Institute for Strategy and Technology
bio: Baruch Fischhoff is a leading expert in decision science, risk analysis, and risk communication who studies how people make decisions under uncertainty and how to effectively communicate risks. His research bridges psychology, engineering, and policy to address real-world problems in public health, climate action, and national security.

name: Mark Kamlet
title: University Professor of Economics and Public Policy
department: Carnegie Mellon Institute for Strategy & Technology
school: Carnegie Mellon Institute for Strategy and Technology
bio: Mark Kamlet is a University Professor of Economics and Public Policy at CMU who served as Provost and chief academic officer from 2000 to 2014, overseeing research, education, and technology commercialization activities. His work focuses on the intersection of technology, economics, policy, and strategy, with particular engagement in technology-enhanced learning and institutional governance.

name: Nadiya Kostyuk
title: Assistant Professor
department: Carnegie Mellon Institute for Strategy and Technology
school: Carnegie Mellon Institute for Strategy and Technology
bio: Nadiya Kostyuk is an Assistant Professor at CMIST whose research focuses on the intersection of international security and technology, with particular attention to cyber power in domestic and international politics. Her methodological expertise spans spatial statistics, mathematical and computational modeling, text analysis, and network analysis.

name: Connor Halloran Phillips
title: Assistant Teaching Professor
department: Carnegie Mellon Institute for Strategy & Technology
school: Carnegie Mellon Institute for Strategy and Technology
bio: Connor Halloran Phillips is an Assistant Teaching Professor at Carnegie Mellon Institute for Strategy & Technology who examines interest groups, parties, legislatures, and elections in the US with a focus on how federalism shapes phenomena such as partisan polarization and voter participation. His current work analyzes interest groups' campaign contributions and ratings of state legislators to understand their engagement strategies and role in promoting polarization at the state level.

name: Harvey Rishikof
title: Visiting Professor
department: Carnegie Mellon Institute for Strategy & Technology
school: Carnegie Mellon Institute for Strategy and Technology
bio: Harvey Rishikof is a visiting professor at CMIST who teaches courses on law and political institutions, with expertise in national security law, cybersecurity policy, and constitutional law. He serves as Director of Policy and Cyber Security Research at the University of Maryland and is senior counsel to the ABA Standing Committee on Law and National Security.

name: Joshua Schwartz
title: Assistant Professor
department: Carnegie Mellon Institute for Strategy & Technology
school: Carnegie Mellon Institute for Strategy and Technology
bio: Joshua Schwartz is an Assistant Professor at CMIST whose research focuses on the effectiveness of military force and technology, public and policymaker support for the use of force, and the global spread of military technology. His work examines topics ranging from armed drone proliferation and counter-terrorism effectiveness to AI in nuclear systems, gender stereotypes in international politics, and the relationship between domestic polarization and foreign policy.

name: Daniel Silverman
title: Assistant Professor
department: Carnegie Mellon Institute for Strategy & Technology
school: Carnegie Mellon Institute for Strategy and Technology
bio: Daniel Silverman is an Assistant Professor of Political Science at CMIST whose research focuses on international security, political psychology, and the politics of the Middle East and wider Islamic world. He is particularly interested in psychological factors including biases and misperceptions that drive conflicts, and how they can be mitigated or leveraged to promote peace.

name: Anne Lambright
title: Professor
department: Department of Languages, Cultures & Applied Linguistics
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Anne Lambright is the Department Head and Paul Mellon Distinguished Professor of Hispanic Studies, leading the Department of Languages, Cultures & Applied Linguistics at Carnegie Mellon. Her work promotes language learning, deepens cultural understanding through literary and cultural studies, and highlights cutting-edge technology-enhanced language learning.

name: Nevine Abraham
title: Associate Teaching Professor
department: Department of Languages, Cultures & Applied Linguistics
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Nevine Abraham's work examines minorities in the intellectual, social and political history of the Middle East and North Africa, focusing on how institutions of power from imperialism to nationalism have marginalized groups and shaped citizenship.
