She mentors students across multiple disciplines, develops courses, and collaborates with various programs to create equitable opportunities for learners to contribute to knowledge and community building.

name: Jane Bernstein
title: Professor of English
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Jane Bernstein is a creative writer whose work spans fiction, memoir, essays, and screenwriting. Her latest novel, The Face Tells the Secret, draws on her experience as a lapsed screenwriter and memoirist, and she has received two NEA Fellowships and a Fulbright Fellowship for her creative work.

name: David Brown
title: Associate Teaching Professor of English
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: David Brown studies writing as a social practice, examining its structures and history across contexts ranging from online discussion boards to sports reportage to educational assessments. His research explores linguistic identity, code-switching, and how literary characters from African diasporic, Chinese, and Indian backgrounds have been represented in British fiction and drama.

name: Janine Carlock
title: Lecturer, Writing & Communication
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Janine Carlock is a lecturer with 28 years of teaching experience across ESL programs, writing centers, and first-year and graduate writing programs. Her research focuses on the science of learning, information literacy, online teaching practices, and best practices for helping students develop writing expertise as a process.

name: Mario Castagnaro
title: Special Faculty
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Mario Castagnaro is a Special Faculty member in the Department of English whose work focuses on professional writing. He teaches courses and conducts research in writing practices for professional contexts.

name: Andrea Comiskey
title: Lecturer
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Andrea Comiskey teaches in the Writing & Communication program and is working on a multimodal book project on the poetics of stop-motion animation, supported by an American Council of Learned Societies fellowship. Her research interests include animation aesthetics, narratology, film and media technologies, and the history of the U.S. film industry.

name: Doug Coulson
title: Associate Professor of English
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Doug Coulson's research explores how legal language shapes justice, identity, and power, with particular focus on rhetoric in judicial writing and fundamental rights decisions. He examines the historical and rhetorical conditions through which language includes and excludes, persuades and silences across law, democracy, and classical rhetoric.

name: Jim Daniels
title: Professor Emeritus of Creative Writing
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Jim Daniels is a Professor Emeritus of Creative Writing who has taught at Carnegie Mellon since 1981, specializing in poetry, fiction, and screenwriting. His work has been widely recognized, including multiple NEA fellowships, the Brittingham Prize for Poetry, and featured publications in Pushcart Prize and Best American Poetry anthologies.

name: Sharon Dilworth
title: Associate Professor of English
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Sharon Dilworth is a mid-career writer whose work explores the small tragedies and emotional resonance of lives in progress, focusing on characters shaped by their landscapes and inescapable pasts. Her fiction and plays capture the ambiguity, paradox, and grace notes of everyday life, drawing on themes of desire, loss, and human muddle-through resilience.

name: Linda Flower
title: Professor Emerita of English
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Linda Flower studies writing as a social-cognitive process and rhetorical problem-solving, with research spanning from individual writing pedagogy to community-based literacy initiatives. Her work examines rhetorical agency, public engagement, and how socially engaged education impacts student outcomes, particularly among marginalized speakers and communities.

name: Emma Fries
title: Arts Greenhouse Head Instructor
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Emma Fries is an artist who works to connect students to the arts as an access point to other areas of learning, focusing on young people who engage in subject matter through art, creative writing, and self-expression. As head instructor, she designs and implements Arts Greenhouse curricula for youth programming throughout the greater Pittsburgh area.

name: Rochel Gasson
title: Special Faculty
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences

name: Barbara George
title: Senior Lecturer
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Barbara George is a teacher and researcher in environmental communication, technical writing, and writing studies pedagogy who explores the intersections of environmental risk, energy discourse, and climate change communication. Her work examines environmental justice, narrative frameworks in environmental deliberation, and student retention through multilingual initiatives and writing support across disciplines.

name: Andrew Gordon
title: Special Faculty
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Andrew Gordon specializes in professional and technical writing, using language and design to bridge communication between experts and non-experts across diverse industries. As an instructor at Carnegie Mellon for over a decade, he has taught hundreds of students professional and technical writing while also working as VP of Creative at Nearby Creative, a Pittsburgh-based software and creative services consulting firm.

name: Jeffrey Hinkelman
title: Senior Lecturer and Director of the Film & Visual Media Program
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Jeffrey Hinkelman is a Senior Lecturer and Director of the Film & Visual Media Program in the Department of English at Carnegie Mellon University. His area of study focuses on film and visual media.

name: Paul Hopper
title: Paul Mellon Distinguished Professor Emeritus of the Humanities
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Paul Hopper's research centers on the connections between rhetoric and grammar, exploring how linguistic structure emerges through repetitions of favored word groupings in discourse rather than being immanent in language. His work includes studies of grammaticalization, comparative Indo-European and Malayo-Polynesian languages, discourse analysis, and human-ape communication, with a critical perspective on standard linguistic assumptions from a rhetorical viewpoint.

name: Alan Houser
title: Special Faculty
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Alan Houser is a Special Faculty member in the Department of English who specializes in professional writing, technical writing, and communication. His work focuses on preparing students for effective written communication in professional and technical contexts.

name: Sarah Hae-In Idzik
title: Assistant Professor of Rhetoric
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Sarah Idzik's work is situated at the intersection of rhetoric, Asian American/ethnic studies, and critical adoption studies, centering on rhetorics of race and racialization, particularly of Asian American and Asian diasporic communities in the U.S. Her dissertation analyzed rhetorics of transnational adoption from Asia to the U.S. from the 1950s to the present, tracing recurring themes of charity, rescue, and paternalism that have contributed to the marginalization of Asian American adoptees.

name: Barbara Johnstone
title: Professor Emeritus of English and Linguistics
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Barbara Johnstone's work is in discourse studies at the intersection of rhetoric, linguistics, and critical theory, examining how relationships between individuals and communities are created and maintained through language and discourse. Her current research focuses on Pittsburghese and how local speech functions as a rhetorical resource, using qualitative research methods such as ethnography and discourse analysis.

name: Alan Kennedy
title: Professor Emeritus of English
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Alan Kennedy studies the art of literature with a focus on how literature communicates through what he calls the rhetoric of literature, particularly in fiction. He emphasizes the performance aspect of reading and the construction of emotion and feeling in narratives.

name: Jon Klancher
title: Professor Emeritus
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Jon Klancher's research focuses on later eighteenth and early nineteenth-century British culture, book and reading history, and the sociology of literature. His recent work explores the emergence of the modern idea of scale from early-modern sciences to nineteenth and twentieth-century disciplines, as exemplified in his award-winning book Transfiguring the Arts and Sciences.

name: Peggy Knapp
title: Professor Emerita of English
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Peggy Knapp specializes in imaginative and argumentative texts from medieval and early modern England, examining them through literary and aesthetic theory. She has published extensively on Chaucer, Shakespeare, and contemporary literature, and founded the annual book series Assays: Critical Approaches to Medieval and Renaissance Texts.

name: Alan Thomas Kohler
title: Senior Lecturer, Writing and Communication
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Alan Thomas Kohler is an accomplished educational leader and communication specialist with 22+ years of experience in higher education, entrepreneurship, and non-profit management. His current research examines the integration of AI into writing, learning, and teaching processes, with additional interests in UX design approaches to classroom learning and teacher training and development.

name: Atesede Makonnen
title: Assistant Professor
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Atesede Makonnen's research focuses on the visualization of race in British culture from early modernity to the nineteenth century, examining how novels, poetry, illustrations, and theatrical practice construct ideas about race across media. Her current project investigates racialized sight in nineteenth-century British culture, exploring how the end of slavery sparked new forms of anti-blackness centered in visuality.

name: Peter Zaragoza Mayshle
title: Lecturer, Writing & Communication
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Peter Zaragoza Mayshle is a creative writer and lecturer whose work explores rhetorics of space, public memory, and ethnography through fiction. His short stories have been published internationally, and he is a finalist for Farrar, Straus & Giroux's inaugural Writer's Fellowship for a novel exploring the first six months of the Duterte presidency in the Philippines.

name: Jane McCafferty
title: Professor of English
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Jane McCafferty is a Professor of English who teaches Fiction Writing, Creative Nonfiction, and Literary Journalism, emphasizing the power of language and storytelling to reshape the world and self. Her published works include four award-winning books and numerous stories and essays featured in prestigious literary journals and on NPR, exploring themes of human connection, redemption, and social class.

name: Laura McCann
title: Lecturer
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Dr. McCann is jointly appointed in English and Biomedical Engineering, studying how humans navigate communicative challenges accompanying medical diagnoses and treatments, with emphasis on rhetorical strategies in mediated peer-to-peer spaces. Her work bridges the gap between technical medical decision-making and the interconnected communicative problems that accompany diagnoses and interventions.

name: Paul Michiels
title: Lecturer
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Paul Michiels is a Lecturer in the Writing and Communication Program at Carnegie Mellon University, teaching first-year writing, professional and technical communication. His research focuses on how expert scholars engage with sources across academic disciplines and what that work suggests for writing instruction.

name: Korryn Mozisek
title: Special Faculty
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Korryn Mozisek's research focuses on the rhetoric of sport, particularly how sport shapes gender and sexuality norms within culture, and on global learning and cultural competency development. She also conducts educational research on reflective practices in learning and serves as Director of Integrative Learning, overseeing programs like the University Lecture Series and educational role-playing initiatives.

name: Kat Myers
title: Special Faculty
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences

name: Chris Neuwirth
title: Professor Emerita
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Chris Neuwirth's research focuses on developing theory- and research-based computer tools for reading and writing, and conducting empirical research on how these tools affect writer performance. Her work explores how external representations can augment writing, with particular emphasis on collaborative writing and policy arguments.

name: Kathy M. Newman
title: Associate Professor of English and Director of Graduate Studies
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Kathy Newman studies the dialectical relationship between mass culture institutions—television, film, radio, and the internet—and social/political formations. Her work combines cultural analysis with labor history, examining representations of class in mid-20th century film and television, and engages critical theory, historical materialism, literature, visual art, and music.

name: Courtney Novosat
title: Senior Lecturer
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Courtney Novosat is a Senior Lecturer and founding co-editor of WOVEN journal, teaching first-year and professional writing with over twenty years of experience. Her research focuses on nineteenth-century American novels examining narratives of race and resistance, particularly in utopian and dystopian texts, while she actively collaborates on writing studies pedagogy projects centered on assignment design and inclusive professional communication.

name: John Oddo
title: Associate Professor of English
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: John Oddo's research examines how powerful agents use language and symbols to generate support for war, focusing on intertextual rhetoric that operates across texts and time. He analyzes how U.S. political leaders reuse rhetorical techniques to manipulate the public and how media institutions recontextualize political claims during the run-up to military conflicts.

name: Julie Pal-Agrawal
title: Lecturer, Writing & Communication
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Julie Pal-Agrawal is a lecturer with over 25 years of experience teaching critical thinking, literature, advertising, and composition. Her research focuses on how self-reflection and journaling empower at-risk youth to advocate for themselves, informed by her work with non-profits and orphans in India.

name: Nupoor Ranade
title: Assistant Professor of Rhetoric and Technical Communication
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Nupoor Ranade is an Assistant Professor of Rhetoric and Technical Communication whose interdisciplinary work bridges humanities and technology, exploring how audiences have evolved from passive information receivers to active participants in knowledge production, particularly in AI systems. Her research addresses ethics, inclusion, and communication in rapidly advancing technologies, with particular focus on AI design, chatbot development, and prompt engineering from a rhetorical perspective.

name: Camille Rankine
title: Assistant Professor of English
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Camille Rankine is a writer and teacher interested in the mechanics of language and storytelling, as well as the systemic mechanisms that shape human existence through race, gender, and nation. Her work investigates identity, inheritance, and first-generation Black American experience through both poetry and non-fiction.

name: Andreea Deciu Ritivoi
title: William S. Dietrich Professor of English
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Andreea Deciu Ritivoi is William S. Dietrich Professor of English at Carnegie Mellon University and co-editor of the narrative studies journal Storyworlds, with research interests in political rhetoric, hermeneutics, narrative studies, and migration. She has authored three books and edited several volumes on topics ranging from personal identity and rhetorical theory to controversy, art, and society.

name: Robyn Rowley
title: Lecturer
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Robyn Rowley's research is situated within feminist media studies with particular attention to the politics of identity, feminist theory, and disability, employing media-archeological approaches to examine technological transformation and digital media production. Her work includes studies of audience formation and engagement, with recent publications on maternal representation in social media and digital culture.

name: Julia Salehzadeh
title: Special Faculty
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Julia Salehzadeh is a Special Faculty member in the Department of English who specializes in writing and communication. Her work focuses on helping students develop effective writing and communication skills.

name: Santiago Sanchez
title: Visiting Assistant Professor
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Santiago Jose Sanchez is a queer Colombian American writer and educator whose fiction has appeared in major publications including McSweeney's and ZYZZYVA. A graduate of the Iowa Writers' Workshop, Sanchez brings extensive teaching experience from institutions including the University of Iowa, Grinnell College, and Oberlin College.

name: Karen Rossi Schnakenberg
title: Teaching Professor Emerita of English
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Karen Rossi Schnakenberg's research focuses on the intersections of course design, professional and technical writing, and the history of writing instruction in higher education. She is particularly interested in how specialized information is communicated to non-expert audiences and the relationship between theory and practice in writing instruction.

name: Lauren Shapiro
title: Associate Professor of English
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Lauren Shapiro is a feminist writer exploring how motherhood, political consciousness, and American culture influence human experience, with particular focus on tonal ranges in poetry and how humor and syntax generate emotional and intellectual responses. Her work engages themes of mental illness, grief, public spectacle, and the quotidian, and she is an accomplished translator of contemporary Latin American and Spanish poetry.

name: David Shumway
title: Professor of English
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: David Shumway researches and teaches American culture and cultural theory, with special interests in film, popular music, and fiction from the late nineteenth to twenty-first century, alongside theoretical concerns in Marxism, media theory, and the politics of literary form. He is the author of several monographs including Rock Star: The Making of Cultural Icons from Elvis to Springsteen and American Narrative Realism, and is currently working on an institutional history of theory and a book on nostalgia.

name: Ed Simon
title: Public Humanities Special Faculty
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Ed Simon is a Public Humanities Lecturer and prolific essayist whose work spans creative writing, literary criticism, and cultural history. He serves as Editor-in-Chief for Belt Magazine and The Pittsburgh Review of Books, with publications in The Atlantic, The Paris Review, The Washington Post, and The New York Times, and is the author of numerous books including Devil's Contract and Pandemonium.

name: Brian Staszel
title: Special Faculty
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Brian Staszel is the Multimedia Designer & Video Director for the Carnegie Mellon University Robotics Institute, creating science documentaries and visual content for PBS and The Discovery Channel. He teaches Introduction to Multimedia Design at CMU and has extensive experience in motion graphics, filmmaking, and interactive media production.

name: Kristina Straub
title: Professor Emerita
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Kristina Straub's research focuses on feminist cultural studies, sexuality studies, and performance in 18th-century Britain, with particular expertise in how gender, labor, and sexuality intersect in historical performance and popular culture. Her work examines the confluence of commercial culture, theater, and the formation of public knowledge, with attention to women's roles as both subjects and active participants in knowledge production.

name: Seth Strickland
title: Lecturer, Writing & Communication
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Seth Strickland is a Lecturer in Writing and Communication in the Department of English at Carnegie Mellon University. He focuses on teaching and developing students' writing and communication skills.

name: Isabelle Strollo
title: Adjunct Instructor
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Isabelle Strollo is an Adjunct Instructor in the Department of English specializing in film and visual media. Her teaching focuses on the analysis and interpretation of cinematic works and visual culture.

name: Tony Swofford
title: Special Faculty
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Anthony Swofford is an acclaimed author of memoirs including Jarhead, which was adapted into a 2005 film, and the novel Exit A. He teaches screenwriting, television writing, and fiction at Carnegie Mellon, bringing expertise in narrative across multiple genres and media.

name: Chad Szalkowski-Ference
title: Special Faculty
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences

name: Steve Twedt
title: Special Faculty
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Steve Twedt is a veteran journalist with four decades of experience at newspapers across the United States, including work on the Pulitzer Prize-winning Mount St. Helens coverage. He has received numerous national journalism honors and has been teaching at Carnegie Mellon since 2005, bringing professional expertise in writing and reporting to the classroom.

name: Ralph Vituccio
title: Teaching Professor
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Ralph Vituccio is a Teaching Professor at CMU's Entertainment Technology Center who creates award-winning interactive projects and documentaries addressing social issues such as sexual assault, racial profiling, and war. His work spans film, virtual reality, and interactive media, with numerous grants from arts councils and festival selections worldwide.

name: Christopher Warren
title: Professor of English and Department Head
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Christopher Warren's research spans digital humanities, law and literature, political theory, early modern literature, print culture, and the history of political thought. His current work uses machine learning and artificial intelligence to discover anonymous printers responsible for clandestine materials in his project "Freedom and the Press before Freedom of the Press."

name: Mariam Wassif
title: Assistant Professor of English
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Mariam Wassif's work focuses on rhetoric, race, and material culture in the long eighteenth century and Romanticism, examining how major authors used classical rhetoric to respond to political, economic, and environmental change. Her current book project, "Poisoned Vestments," traces the encounter between ancient rhetoric and commercial modernity across canonical genres including poetry, drama, satire, and prose, adopting feminist and decolonial perspectives.

name: Danielle Zawodny Wetzel
title: Teaching Professor; Director of Writing & Communication Program
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Danielle Zawodny Wetzel focuses on teacher training, curriculum design, and assessment for first-year writing, particularly for linguistically diverse students. She works to design instruction that helps students make data-informed, mindful, and culturally aware writing decisions while developing rhetorical reasoning skills.

name: Rebecca Wigginton
title: Lecturer
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Rebecca Wigginton researches Victorian psychiatry and selfhood in nineteenth-century novels, gothic and ecogothic literature, and science fiction. Her current book project explores the Victorian sleepwalker as an embodiment of consciousness models in medical, literary, and judicial contexts.

name: Jeffrey Williams
title: Professor of English and of Literary and Cultural Studies
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Jeffrey Williams is a scholar of contemporary US fiction and literary and cultural studies who examines what it means to be contemporary through analyses of recent generations and their influence on literature and culture. He is also a leading figure in Critical University Studies, having researched and written extensively about contemporary higher education, student debt, and the role of criticism as a live public practice.

name: Stephen Wittek
title: Associate Professor of English and Director of Literary and Cultural Studies
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Stephen Wittek's work lies at the intersection of early modern drama, cultural studies, and digital humanities, with recent scholarship examining Shakespeare's engagement with conversion in post-Reformation England and the relationship between commercial news markets and theater. He directs the Shakespeare-VR project and co-directs the Center for Early Modern Print, Networks, and Performance, combining traditional literary scholarship with innovative digital pedagogy.

name: Joanna Wolfe
title: Teaching Professor of English
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Her research focuses on equity and communication, examining factors that inhibit participation of women and underrepresented minorities in cultures of power while investigating evidence-based strategies for overcoming these inequities. She also conducts applied research on technical communication, writing in the disciplines (particularly engineering), and how technological affordances change the ways we read, write, and teach.

name: Heidi Wright
title: Senior Lecturer, Writing & Communication
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Heidi Wright is an applied linguist who teaches and researches academic writing and reading, developing materials to help university students meet writing challenges. She uses quantitative and qualitative corpus linguistics techniques to examine lexical, grammatical, and discoursal patterns, with current research focused on literature reviews across multiple disciplines.

name: James Wynn
title: Professor of English
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: James Wynn is a Professor of English and director of the Rhetoric Program at CMU, where his research explores science, mathematics, and public policy from a rhetorical perspective. He has published books on mathematical argument in science and citizen science in the digital age, and teaches courses in rhetoric of science, science communication, and technical writing.

name: Jungwan Yoon
title: Senior Lecturer, Writing & Communication
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Jungwan Yoon is a researcher and teacher in second language education and academic writing with a PhD in Applied Linguistics from Penn State. Her research focuses on academic literacy development, corpus-based analysis of academic discourse, and how writers use language to construct meaning in academic contexts, particularly examining the interface between lexico-grammatical elements and genre features.

name: John R Anderson
title: Richard King Mellon University Professor of Psychology and Computer Science
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: John Anderson's research aims to understand the structure of higher-level cognition through unified theories of cognition, particularly focusing on mathematical problem solving using the ACT-R cognitive architecture. His current work analyzes brain imaging data to test cognitive models and develop instruction that helps students extend their knowledge beyond taught procedures.

name: Jennifer Bruder
title: Associate Teaching Professor
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Jennifer Bruder is an associate teaching professor of psychology at Carnegie Mellon University in Qatar who teaches courses related to cognition, neurobiology, and research methods. Her research focuses on neurodevelopmental disorders such as autism and dyslexia, as well as environmental and sustainable psychology issues in Qatar and the Gulf region.

name: Jocelyn Bélanger
title: Associate Teaching Professor
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Dr. Jocelyn Bélanger is an Associate Teaching Professor of Psychology at Carnegie Mellon University in Qatar who specializes in the psychology of terrorism, radicalization, and deradicalization. He directs the CMUQ Violent Extremism and Environmental Sustainability Lab and has published over 100 works on motivated reasoning, self-sacrifice, and environmental psychology.

name: Sharon Carver
title: Professor of Psychology (teaching), Emeritus
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: As a learning scientist, Sharon Carver aims to bridge the domains of developmental and educational psychology by combining cognitive task analysis, instructional design, and focused assessment to enhance teaching and learning. As emeritus director of the Carnegie Mellon Children's School, her research focused on supporting researchers and collaborating with educators to improve educational outcomes.

name: Chante Cox-Boyd
title: Professor of Psychology (Teaching)
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Cox-Boyd's research focuses on social cognition and how individuals make social judgments about others based on minimal information, particularly in the context of race, ethnicity, and stereotyping. Her work explores the sources of discrimination, the impact of name stereotypes on minority groups, and the role of self-fulfilling prophecies in achievement and emotional health.

name: Kasey Creswell
title: Associate Professor of Psychology
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Kasey Creswell is interested in understanding the mechanisms underlying the development and maintenance of addiction, with a focus on affective and cognitive mechanisms of cigarette craving and alcohol use. Her research emphasizes the importance of social and biological factors in addiction etiology and uses experimental methodologies including in vivo cue exposure paradigms and longitudinal designs with clinical samples.

name: Crista N. Crittenden
title: Lecturer
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Dr. Crittenden is a Lecturer in Psychology with clinical focus on the mental health and well-being of undergraduate and graduate student populations. Her research interests include the impact of social factors on physical health, particularly lung function.

name: Janine Dutcher
title: Assistant Professor (Research)
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Janine Dutcher's research seeks to understand the neurobiological mechanisms of stress reduction interventions using neuroimaging, psychoneuroimmunology, and ecological momentary assessment. She conducts translational work investigating how these interventions improve mental and physical health, particularly among clinical and at-risk groups.

name: Emilia Ezrina
title: Visiting Lecturer
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Emilia Ezrina is interested in cognitive psychology with a focus on bilingualism and multilingualism, examining how these linguistic capabilities relate to other cognitive processes like decision-making and emotion. She uses demonstrations and mock experiments in her classes to illustrate complex cognitive mechanisms and engage students in understanding how the mind processes language and other fundamental cognitive functions.

name: Brooke Feeney
title: Professor of Psychology
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Brooke Feeney's research examines how close relationships and social interactions facilitate human thriving across the lifespan, using detailed observations and physiological assessments of dyadic interactions. Her work spans multiple relationship types and stages of life, investigating how social behaviors and relationship patterns impact mental health, physical health, resilience, and life satisfaction.

name: Susanne Ferber
title: Professor and Department Head
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Dr. Ferber's research focuses on the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying awareness of perception using diverse methodological approaches including fMRI and EEG imaging studies. She has published over 70 papers investigating cognitive impairments in neurological patients and has extensive expertise in cognitive neuroscience.

name: Anna Fisher
title: Associate Professor of Psychology
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Anna Fisher studies how young children learn and generalize knowledge, with a focus on the development of uniquely human modes of learning and category-based inductive reasoning. Her research explores the relationship between sustained attention and learning, with applications to improving children's learning in academic settings.

name: Vicki Helgeson
title: Professor of Psychology
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Vicki Helgeson is a Professor of Psychology whose research focuses on how people adjust to chronic illnesses such as heart disease, cancer, and diabetes, examining the roles of personality traits, social relationships, and gender-related characteristics. She has conducted longitudinal studies spanning over 25 years investigating psychological adjustment, physical health outcomes, and support interventions for individuals and couples managing chronic disease.

name: Kenneth R. Koedinger
title: Hillman Professor of Computer Science, Professor of Human-Computer Interaction and Psychology
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Kenneth Koedinger is a professor studying human learning and educational technology design, with expertise in cognitive modeling and cognitive tutors that support student learning in problem-solving environments. He leads LearnLab, the Pittsburgh Science of Learning Center, which uses computational approaches to identify instructional conditions that promote robust student learning across mathematics, science, and language domains.

name: Phoebe Lam
title: Assistant Professor
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Phoebe Lam studies how stress experienced during early life shapes psychosocial and biological development across the lifespan and identifies protective factors that reduce negative health consequences of chronic stress. Her lab uses longitudinal studies, mega-analyses, and meta-analyses to understand stress-related disease risks including cardiovascular, respiratory, and cancer diseases.

name: Marsha Lovett
title: Professor of Psychology (Teaching)
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Marsha Lovett studies how learning works in college-level courses and develops interventions to improve student learning across disciplines including physics, statistics, and engineering. She has created innovative educational technologies such as StatTutor and the Learning Dashboard, and co-authored the book How Learning Works, which translates learning research into principles for effective teaching.

name: Brian MacWhinney
title: Teresa Heinz Professor of Cognitive Psychology
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Brian MacWhinney studies language acquisition as an emergent process grounded in competitive Darwinian mechanisms across phylogenetic, ontogenetic, and synchronic time scales. His research uses the Competition Model framework to understand how children master language through exposure to patterns in adult input, and how perspective-taking and brain mechanisms underlie grammatical emergence.

name: Kody Manke-Miller
title: Associate Professor of Psychology (Teaching)
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Kody Manke-Miller studies social justice and inequality through the lens of social psychology, focusing on how social identity and perceptions of social situations affect educational outcomes and wellbeing. He runs the Data Driven Diversity Lab and conducts intervention research examining stereotype threat, self-affirmation, and evidence-based strategies to improve student thriving at CMU.

name: Abigail Noyce
title: Assistant Research Professor
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Abigail Noyce is an Assistant Research Professor affiliated with the Neuroscience Institute whose research focuses on cognitive neuroscience, learning science, and perception. Her work employs behavioral methods and non-invasive brain monitoring techniques to investigate auditory processing, executive control, memory, and spatial cognition.

name: David Rakison
title: Associate Professor of Psychology
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: David Rakison studies how infants develop mental representations and concepts of objects and events around them, examining when and how they categorize objects, learn about motion properties of animates and inanimates, and how action affects conceptual development. He employs behavioral methodologies with infants, studies conceptual development in children with autism, and develops computational models to understand the mechanisms of perceptual and concept development in early childhood.

name: Michael J Tarr
title: Kavčić-Moura Professor of Cognitive and Brain Science
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Michael Tarr's research focuses on how the primate brain converts 2D retinal images into the perception of objects and scenes. His work spans face and object perception, perceptual expertise, visual categorization, computational vision systems, and novel neuroimaging methods including real-time adaptive fMRI.

name: Erik Thiessen
title: Associate Professor of Psychology
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Erik Thiessen studies learning mechanisms in infancy and childhood, with a focus on how infants use statistical information to acquire language and other complex knowledge. His research explores the similarities and differences between infant and adult learning, and examines how characteristics of input and prior experience facilitate or impair learning across linguistic and non-linguistic domains.

name: Michael Trujillo
title: Assistant Professor of Psychology
school: Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences
bio: Michael Trujillo's research bridges social psychology of stigma and psychobiological pathways linking social stress to health outcomes, with a focus on stigmatized populations. He uses experimental and non-experimental approaches including ecological momentary assessment and longitudinal methods to investigate physiological, affective, and behavioral mechanisms connecting stigma and health, primarily among LGBTQ individuals and other minorities.

name: Jack Chow
title: Distinguished Service Professor
bio: Distinguished Service Professor Jack Chow is a former U.S. Ambassador who served as the first Assistant Director-General of the World Health Organization on HIV/AIDS, Tuberculosis, and Malaria. His research and teaching focus on global health diplomacy, infectious disease policy, and the design of national strategic policies.

name: Karen B. Clay
title: Teresa and H. John Heinz III Professor of Economics and Public Policy
department: Heinz College of Information Systems and Public Policy
bio: Karen Clay is an economist studying the relationship between environmental pollution and public health outcomes, with particular focus on air quality, lead exposure, and their long-term effects on mortality and economic development. Her research combines historical analysis with contemporary policy evaluation to understand how environmental regulations impact population health and economic outcomes.

name: Avinash (Avi) Collis
title: Assistant Professor
department: Heinz College of Information Systems and Public Policy
bio: Avinash Collis is an Assistant Professor studying the economic implications of information technologies. His research has been published in leading academic journals and covered extensively in major media outlets and policy reports.

name: Brett Ashley Crawford, PhD
title: Teaching Professor
bio: Dr. Crawford is a Teaching Professor of Arts Management at Carnegie Mellon's Heinz College with extensive professional experience as an arts manager in theatre. Her research focuses on creative theories, marketing, audience engagement, technology solutions for performing arts institutions, and the history of American theatre.

name: George T. Duncan
title: Professor of Statistics, Emeritus
bio: George Duncan is an emeritus professor whose research focuses on Bayesian decision making and the confidentiality of statistical databases, addressing the tension between privacy and information access. He is a Fellow of the American Statistical Association, the American Association for the Advancement of Science, and the Royal Statistical Society, with extensive contributions to privacy policy and statistical disclosure limitation.

name: Rebekah Fitzsimmons
title: Associate Teaching Professor
bio: Dr. Fitzsimmons is an Associate Teaching Professor of Professional Communication who specializes in innovative digital pedagogies and helping experts communicate technical knowledge to non-experts through storytelling. Her research focuses on digital humanities analysis of children's and young adult literature, including stylometric analysis and network analysis of historical canon-forming documents.

name: Andrew Garin
title: Assistant Professor of Economics
bio: Dr. Garin's research leverages administrative datasets and quasi-experimental methodologies to study labor economics, focusing on how firm behavior and labor market structure drive economic inequality and mobility. He is particularly interested in the future of work and how policy can promote opportunity in 21st century labor markets.

name: Martin S. Gaynor
title: University Professor of Economics and Public Policy, Emeritus
bio: Martin Gaynor is an Emeritus University Professor whose research focuses on competition and incentives in health care markets and antitrust policy. He is a founder of the Health Care Cost Institute and an elected member of the National Academy of Medicine, having advised governments and testified before Congress on health care competition issues.

name: Rayid Ghani
title: Distinguished Career Professor
bio: Rayid Ghani is a Distinguished Career Professor who works to increase the use of AI, machine learning, and data science in solving public policy and social challenges in a fair and equitable manner. He collaborates with governments and non-profits on policy areas including health, criminal justice, education, public safety, and economic development, and founded the Data Science for Social Good Fellowship.

name: William Gogolak
title: Assistant Teaching Professor
bio: Will Gogolak is an Assistant Teaching Professor with over a decade of experience in financial markets, financial planning, and financial modeling across consulting roles. His primary research focus is on predictive modeling in commodity markets and corporate finance, with an emphasis on applying classroom concepts to real-world financial problems.

name: Christopher Goranson
title: Distinguished Service Professor, Senior Director for the School of Public Policy and Management
department: School of Public Policy and Management
bio: Christopher Goranson is a Distinguished Service Professor and Senior Director for the School of Public Policy and Management who teaches courses in GIS, data visualization, and the intersection of design, innovation, and analytics. He brings extensive experience from federal government service, including his role at 18F managing digital services consulting and strategy projects, and as a Presidential Innovation Fellow working on government modernization and user-centered design initiatives.

name: Daniel Green, Ph.D.
title: Distinguished Service Professor/Director
department: Master of Entertainment Industry Management Program
bio: Daniel Green is a Distinguished Service Professor and Director of the Master of Entertainment Industry Management program, with extensive experience in television production, film directing, and theatre direction. His research focuses on arts administration, cultural management, higher education leadership, and the intersection of artificial intelligence with creative industries.

name: Amelia M. Haviland
title: E.J. Barone Professor of Health Systems Management
bio: Dr. Haviland's research focuses on causal analysis with observational data and analysis of longitudinal and complex survey data applied to policy issues in health and criminology. Her work includes assessing the effects of health insurance plans on costs and disparities, exploring health disparities mechanisms, and extending statistical methods like group-based trajectory modeling to address causal questions in criminal justice.

name: Wanda Heading-Grant
title: Distinguished Service Professor
department: Heinz College of Information Systems and Public Policy
bio: Dr. Wanda Heading-Grant is a Vice Provost for Community, Culture and Engagement at Carnegie Mellon University with 30 years of experience in higher education leadership focused on advancing inclusive excellence. Her career spans academic and administrative roles where she has established programs, policies, and practices to foster institutional change and equity.

name: Larry Heimann
title: Teaching Professor
bio: Larry Heimann's research focuses on organizational decision making, particularly using game theory and computer simulations to assess decisions in network security. His current work investigates how cybercriminals make decisions about joining teams and coordinating activities through the lens of security economics.

name: Dr. Kim J. Hyatt
title: Professor
bio: Professor Hyatt is Executive Director of the Heinz XR Lab and explores how generative AI and multi-user mixed reality experiences can transform professional communication, including avatar-based relationship building and immersive storytelling. She also works as an educational technology consultant and founder of Team RobotiX, a nonprofit connecting K-12 students with Pittsburgh professionals through STEAM initiatives.

name: Mark S. Kamlet
title: University Professor of Economics and Public Policy; Provost Emeritus
department: Department of Social and Decision Sciences
bio: Mark Kamlet is University Professor of Economics and Public Policy and Provost Emeritus at Carnegie Mellon with joint appointments across multiple departments. His research and leadership focus on economics, public policy, decision sciences, and the intersection of technology with education and institutional strategy.

name: Jonathan Kanter
title: Distinguished Service Professor
bio: Jonathan Kanter served as Assistant Attorney General for the Antitrust Division at the U.S. Department of Justice from 2021-2024, leading landmark enforcement efforts across major sectors including technology, healthcare, and media. He brings extensive expertise in antitrust law, regulatory policy, and government enforcement, having previously been a partner at a top international law firm and founder of his own antitrust boutique.

name: Felix Koenig
title: Assistant Professor of Economics
bio: Felix Koenig is an Assistant Professor of Economics whose research explores how wages are set, how firms hire, and how new technologies reshape work and earnings. His work uses large-scale administrative data, randomized controlled trials, and modern causal inference methods to examine wage-setting tools, non-wage job attributes, and technological drivers of labor market inequality.

name: Brian K. Kovak
title: Professor of Economics and Public Policy
department: Heinz College of Information Systems and Public Policy
bio: Brian Kovak is a Professor of Economics and Public Policy whose research examines the labor market impacts of international trade, migration, and technological change. His recent work focuses on the long-run effects of trade liberalization on workers' employment and earnings, geographic mobility in response to economic shocks, and policies to support displaced workers.

name: David M. Krackhardt
title: Professor
bio: David Krackhardt is a Professor of Organizations at Carnegie Mellon's Heinz College who specializes in network analysis and its application to understanding organizational functioning. His research focuses on cognitive social structures, network diffusion, organizational change leverage points, and the role of strong ties in organizations.

name: Kristen S. Kurland
title: University Professor of Architecture, Information Systems, and Public Policy
bio: Kristen Kurland is a University Professor whose work focuses on interdisciplinary collaborations in health, the built environment, geospatial analysis, and 3D data visualization. She leads projects addressing machine learning, artificial intelligence, big data, economic development, smart cities, and urban design in collaboration with academic, healthcare, government, and industry organizations.

name: Chris Labash
title: Associate Professor of Communication and Innovation
bio: Chris Labash is an Associate Professor of Communication and Innovation who studies the mitigation of misinformation and disinformation through principles of nudge theory, behavioral design, and communication techniques. He directs ConsultingLab, a research lab conducting consulting engagements with organizations, and is faculty at CMU's Center for Informed Democracy and Social-cybersecurity (IDeaS), where his current research explores how AI conversations can help people recognize and reject false information and conspiracy theories.

name: Lindsay Larson
title: Assistant Professor of Organizational Behavior
bio: Lindsay's research examines how advanced technologies are transforming team dynamics and work relationships, integrating perspectives from organizational behavior, psychology, communication, and human-computer interaction. She focuses on interpersonal functioning in technologically-advanced teams, including human-AI collaboration and geographically distributed science teams.

name: David Lassman
title: Distinguished Service Professor of Organizational Management
bio: David Lassman is a Distinguished Service Professor with over 25 years of operations and engineering management experience across diverse industries. He teaches organizational behavior, leadership, and strategy, with a focus on culture, change management, and organizational effectiveness.

name: Beibei Li
title: Professor of IT and Management
bio: Beibei Li's research investigates the intersection of social and technical aspects of information technology, focusing on how human decision-making interacts with technological disruptions in markets. She applies interdisciplinary approaches combining AI, machine learning, economics, and randomized experiments to design effective strategies for technology platforms and policymakers.

name: Ari Lightman
title: Distinguished Service Professor
bio: Ari Lightman is a Distinguished Service Professor at Carnegie Mellon's Heinz College who teaches classes on assessing the impact of emerging technologies, including Digital Transformation and Measuring Social. With 20 years of experience in technology development and commercialization, he directs the CIO Institute and works with industry partners on data-driven strategies across healthcare, telecom, and consumer sectors.

name: Kirsten Martin
title: H. John Heinz III Dean
department: Heinz College of Information Systems and Public Policy
bio: Kirsten Martin's research focuses on the ethics of technology, privacy, and corporate responsibility, with particular attention to algorithmic accountability and the role of business in shaping ethical data practices. She has provided expert testimony to major government agencies including the FTC and National Academies, and has published extensively on privacy, data ethics, and the ethical implications of emerging technologies.

name: Karl Maschino
title: Distinguished Service Professor of Public Policy and Management
bio: Karl Maschino is a Distinguished Service Professor of Public Policy and Management who brings extensive experience from his decade-long tenure as Chief Financial and Administrative Officer of the U.S. Government Accountability Office. His research and teaching focus on federal budget policy, organizational management, and the practical implementation of policy within government institutions.

name: Haylee Massaro
title: Associate Teaching Professor
department: Heinz College of Information Systems and Public Policy
bio: Haylee Massaro is an Associate Teaching Professor who teaches graduate-level courses in writing and communications with research focused on communication best practices in Information Systems Management and emerging ideas in Generative AI. Her background spans education, arts management, military education, and freelance writing and editing.

name: Anna Mayo
title: Anna Loomis McCandless Assistant Professor of Organizational Behavior
bio: Mayo's research explores what characterizes and supports effective teamwork in modern organizations, particularly in dynamic settings where teams form quickly and members change over time. She studies teams in both laboratory and field settings using multiple methods, examining how organizations can maintain coordination and performance amid volatile environments and fluid team membership.

name: Michael McCarthy
title: Associate Teaching Professor of Information Systems
bio: Michael McCarthy is an Associate Teaching Professor of Information Systems at Heinz College with over twenty years of teaching experience in cryptography, distributed systems, and IoT. His research interests focus on data representation and processing methods for the Web of Things, Web Services, and Semantic Web, with extensive experience supervising student projects in blockchain, APIs, and enterprise systems.

name: Sarah Mendelson
title: Distinguished Service Professor of Public Policy
bio: Sarah Mendelson is a Distinguished Service Professor of Public Policy and Director of Sustainable Futures at CMU, focusing on local and global challenges including socioeconomic needs and demographic disparities through the Sustainable Development Goals framework. Her work leverages AI to close data gaps and collaborates with global partners to design people-centered data systems that drive social impact and inform policy solutions.

name: Joseph Mertz
title: Teaching Professor
department: Heinz College of Information Systems and Public Policy
bio: Joseph Mertz is a Teaching Professor and Director of the undergraduate Information Systems Program who specializes in developing technical-professional leadership skills and using technology for development. Since 1998, he has led technology consulting initiatives that have engaged students in helping over 300 organizations domestically and sent over 100 students to developing countries to assist government ministries and NGOs in improving their strategic use of technology.

name: James M. Richardson
title: Distinguished Executive in Residence
bio: Lieutenant General Richardson is a Distinguished Executive in Residence at Carnegie Mellon University with four decades of applied leadership experience in the US Army, including oversight of Army Futures Command and the Army's Artificial Intelligence Integration Center at CMU. He works with university leadership to enhance Carnegie Mellon's thought leadership in transformational research and policy development in global technologies, particularly artificial intelligence.

name: J. David Riel
title: Distinguished Service Professor
bio: David Riel's research focuses on technology application to education, including Web 2.0, learning management systems, and AI/ML, as well as active learning methodologies such as flipped classrooms and team-based learning. His work also examines digital disruption through emergent technologies like IoT, blockchain, and intelligent automation in both business and academic contexts.

name: Stacy Rosenberg
title: Associate Teaching Professor
department: School of Public Policy & Management and School of Information Systems & Management
bio: Stacy Rosenberg is an Associate Teaching Professor at Carnegie Mellon University's Heinz College who specializes in teaching communication courses including Writing for Public Policy, Professional Speaking, and Strategic Presentation Skills. Her research interests focus on the interrelationship between business, government, and society, with particular expertise in political communication, crisis communication, and media representation.

name: Denise M. Rousseau
title: H.J. Heinz II University Professor of Organizational Behavior and Public Policy
department: Heinz College of Information Systems and Public Policy
bio: Denise Rousseau is a leading scholar in organizational behavior whose research focuses on the employment relationship, worker well-being, and organizational effectiveness. She is recognized for developing the theory of the psychological contract and for advancing evidence-based management practices that benefit workers, employers, and stakeholders.

name: William H. Sanders
title: Dean & Professor
bio: William Sanders is a leader in secure and dependable computing research with a focus on critical infrastructures, smart grid resilience, and resiliency metrics and evaluation. He has published over 270 technical papers and directed major research institutes including the Information Trust Institute and Coordinated Science Laboratory.

name: Ananya Sen
title: Assistant Professor
bio: Ananya Sen is an Assistant Professor at Carnegie Mellon University's Heinz College whose research focuses on digital platforms, particularly in media and innovation within the broader digital economy. He employs empirical techniques including field experiments and observational data analysis to investigate economic questions in these domains.

name: Michael Simko
title: Assistant Teaching Professor of Applied AI and Data Science
department: Heinz College of Information Systems and Public Policy
bio: Michael Simko is an Assistant Teaching Professor of Applied AI and Data Science at Carnegie Mellon University's Heinz College with extensive experience as a practicing data scientist across diverse industries including steel, manufacturing, chemicals, and energy. His expertise spans exploratory data analysis, automated analytics pipelines, advanced data visualization, and predictive model development, which he teaches through a practical, accessible approach grounded in real-world problem-solving.

name: Michael D. Smith
title: Professor
school: Heinz College and Tepper School of Business
bio: Michael D. Smith is a Professor of Information Technology and Public Policy who uses economic and statistical techniques to analyze firm and consumer behavior in online markets, particularly for digital information and media products. His research has been published in leading management, economics, and marketing journals and covered extensively in outlets including Harvard Business Review, The Economist, and The Wall Street Journal.

name: David Steier
title: Distinguished Service Professor
department: School of Information Systems and Management
bio: David Steier is a Distinguished Service Professor who teaches courses on data science, analytics management, and artificial intelligence. His research focuses on data-driven approaches to behavioral change, particularly in health and wellness applications.

name: Jillian Stephenson
title: Assistant Teaching Professor
bio: Jillian Stephenson is an Assistant Teaching Professor at Heinz College and a Certified Public Accountant with extensive experience in accounting and financial reporting. She has worked at PricewaterhouseCoopers and Belk Inc., specializing in SEC filings and compliance matters.

name: Robert P. Strauss
title: Professor
bio: Professor Strauss is a tenured full professor of economics and public policy at Carnegie Mellon's Heinz College, where he has been faculty since 1979. His research focuses on public economics, human resources, and urban policy issues.

name: Laura W. Synnott
title: Associate Teaching Professor
bio: Laura Synnott is an Associate Teaching Professor who teaches project management and strategy development to graduate students across Carnegie Mellon University, with specialized courses for physicians in the Master of Medical Management program. She brings over 35 years of executive management and consulting experience in health care, including hospital operations, organizational strategy, and governance.

name: Janusz Szczypula
title: Teaching Professor of Information and Decision Systems
bio: Janusz Szczypula is a Teaching Professor at Heinz College with over 25 years of faculty experience, including serving as Director of the Master of Information Systems Management program. His research focuses on database management, information systems, forecasting methods, and the design of virtual learning environments for knowledge creation.

name: Lowell Taylor
title: H. John Heinz III University Professor of Economics
bio: Lowell Taylor is the H. John Heinz III University Professor of Economics at Heinz College, where he has been faculty since 1990. His research focuses on labor economics and economic demography, with studies examining public education, intergenerational mobility, racial differences in mortality, and healthcare market efficiency.

name: Rahul Telang
title: Trustees Professor of Information Systems
bio: Rahul Telang researches the digital media industry, examining how digitization of content affects incentives for providers and distributors, with particular focus on piracy and copyright policy. He also studies the economics of information security and privacy, investigating vendor incentives, vulnerability disclosure, and the impact of data breach disclosure laws.

name: Randall Trzeciak
title: Adjunct Faculty, MSISPM Program Director
department: School of Information Systems and Management
bio: Randall Trzeciak is the Director of the Masters of Science Information Security Policy & Management Program and an adjunct professor at Heinz College, while also serving as Technical Manager of CERT's Enterprise Threat and Vulnerability Management Team. His expertise spans insider threat detection, incident response, threat analysis, and building organizational security programs to improve security posture and prevent illicit activity.

name: Mustafa Akan
title: Associate Professor
bio: Mustafa Akan is an Associate Professor of Operations Management whose research focuses on pricing, revenue management, and mechanism design with applications to healthcare systems and service operations. His work combines stochastic modeling and queueing theory to address practical problems in healthcare delivery and matching markets.

name: Alberto Alcaraz Aguilar
bio: Alberto Alcaraz Aguilar is the Process and Continuous Improvement Deputy Director at Cinépolis, responsible for standardizing operational business models across 14 countries. His expertise spans industrial engineering, quality systems, and organizational strategy, with 12+ years of teaching experience in process management and continuous improvement.

name: Anna Andree
title: Capstone Advisor
bio: Anna Andree is a research professional with fourteen years of entertainment industry experience, including roles at NBCUniversal and Variety Business Intelligence focused on research and analytics. She advises Carnegie Mellon MEIM students on capstone projects while leveraging her expertise in entertainment business intelligence and data-driven decision making.

name: Linda Argote
title: David M. Kirr and Barbara A. Kirr Professor of Organizational Behavior and Theory
bio: Linda Argote is a distinguished professor studying organizational learning, knowledge management, and innovation. She directs the Center of Organizational Learning, Innovation and Knowledge at Carnegie Mellon University.

name: Jonathan Baker
title: Adjunct Professor
bio: Jonathan Baker is a two-time Emmy-nominated producer and artist known for independent feature films and documentaries, with extensive experience in film marketing and production across major studios. He has taught film economics as an adjunct professor at Carnegie Mellon and the University of Bologna for seventeen years.

name: Joseph J. Balestreire
title: Adjunct Faculty
bio: Joseph J. Balestreire is the Senior Vice President and Manager of Retail Bank Quality and Service Delivery at PNC Bank, with over 25 years of experience in Management and Organization Development across Finance and Healthcare sectors. He has extensive expertise in quality improvement, performance enhancement, and organizational transformation, with a background in both healthcare and financial services.

name: Olivia Barton
bio: Olivia Barton is Music Counsel at Epic Games, where she works on music strategy for interactive entertainment and the Unreal Engine. Previously, she worked in sync licensing at Sony Music, representing major artists and catalogs for film, television, and gaming applications.

name: Dareen Basma
title: Associate Dean of Strategic Initiatives & Student Success
bio: Dr. Dareen Basma is an educator, researcher, and clinician whose work centers on social advocacy, institutional transformation, and community engagement. Her career bridges practice and strategy, with a focus on building systems that foster belonging, well-being, and equitable opportunity in higher education.

name: Tina Batra Hershey
title: Adjunct Professor
bio: Tina Batra Hershey is an adjunct professor and health care attorney specializing in legal and ethical issues in health care delivery and public health. Her research focuses on health care compliance, regulatory matters, and public health preparedness and response.

name: Alisa Ben
title: SVP and Head of Analytics
bio: Alisa Ben is a seasoned executive with extensive experience in strategy, consumer insights, marketing, data analytics, and leadership innovation. She has held senior positions at major media and music companies, pioneering data-driven strategies for product development and artist management globally.

name: Jamie Callan
title: Professor of Computer Science
bio: Jamie Callan is a Professor at the Language Technologies Institute with a joint appointment in Heinz College's School of Information Systems & Management. His research focuses on text-based information retrieval and analysis, including advanced search engine architectures, federated search, adaptive information filtering, and text mining.

name: Amanda Cash
title: Adjunct Faculty
bio: Dr. Amanda Cash is an epidemiologist and Chief Data Officer at the Administration for Community Living, leading evaluation and research efforts for programs serving older adults and people with disabilities. With 17 years of experience at HHS agencies, she has directed divisions focused on health care delivery, evidence evaluation, and data policy, with expertise in chronic disease prevention and health equity.

name: Joshua Centor
title: Adjunct Faculty
bio: Joshua Centor serves as Director of Athletics, Physical Education and Recreation at Carnegie Mellon University, overseeing intercollegiate athletics, club sports, intramurals, and campus recreation. He teaches graduate courses on higher education administration and brings extensive experience from the NCAA national office and higher education management.

name: Tony Cheesebrough
title: Adjunct Faculty
bio: Tony Cheesebrough is Chief Economist for CISA at the U.S. Department of Homeland Security, applying economic analysis and quantitative methods to inform federal policy and resource allocation decisions. His expertise spans benefit-cost analysis, economic impact modeling, risk analysis, and policy evaluation for national security and infrastructure protection.

name: Alex Chisholm
title: Adjunct Faculty
bio: Alex Chisholm is a data professional with over 15 years of experience coding in Python and R to build products and help organizations make evidence-based decisions. Currently serving as Product Manager for Data Science Cloud Solutions at Posit PBC, he brings global experience from technology and education industries while teaching part-time to help students develop practical career-advancing skills.

name: Mukul Chopra
title: Adjunct Faculty
bio: Mukul Chopra is an experienced technology leader with over two decades of CIO and digital transformation experience across prominent organizations. He is a thought leader in change management, cyber-security, and business transformation with expertise in digital strategy, product development, and data analytics.

name: David J. Dausey
title: Distinguished Service Professor
bio: Dr. Dausey is an internationally respected public health and healthcare expert who has led externally funded research projects totaling nearly ten million dollars and authored more than 100 scholarly publications. His work focuses on public health policy, disease surveillance, pandemic preparedness, and healthcare quality, with extensive collaboration with international organizations, federal agencies, and state and local health departments.

name: John Davis
title: Adjunct Faculty
bio: John Davis is an experienced agile practitioner with nearly 20 years in commercial product development who teaches Agile Methods at CMU Heinz. He specializes in developing people, teams, and organizations through the application of agile values, principles, and methodologies.

name: Steve Davis
title: Adjunct Faculty
bio: Dr. Davis is the Chief Operating Officer at Hillcrest Hospital and a staff physician at Cleveland Clinic Children's Hospital with extensive experience in pediatric critical care and healthcare operations. He has published numerous articles on pediatric cardiac surgery and critical care medicine while serving in leadership roles across medical operations and regional healthcare administration.

name: Mark F. DeSantis
title: Adjunct Professor
bio: Mark F. DeSantis is a serial tech entrepreneur and CEO of Bloomfield, an AI company in agriculture, with extensive experience founding and leading venture-backed companies in AI, energy, and online education. He brings decades of entrepreneurial expertise and policy experience to his teaching, having previously held roles in government, venture capital, and management consulting.

name: Dana S. Deasy
title: Faculty, Chief Information Officer (CIO) Certificate Program
bio: Dana Deasy is a globally experienced Chief Information Officer who has led large complex technology organizations across aerospace, automotive, financial services, and government sectors. He served as CIO of the U.S. Department of Defense managing a $47B budget and 100,000+ technologists, and previously held senior technology leadership positions at J.P. Morgan Chase and BP.

name: Jane Downing
title: Adjunct Faculty
department: Heinz College of Information Systems and Public Policy

name: Casey Droege
title: Executive Director
bio: Casey Droege is an artist and cultural producer who has built a two-decade career in the arts while working as an artist, educator, and serial entrepreneur. Through Casey Droege Cultural Productions, she experiments with platforms to introduce artists to the public while building community through entrepreneurship and conceptual art practices.

name: Neelam Dwivedi
title: Adjunct Professor
department: Heinz College of Information Systems and Public Policy
bio: Neelam Dwivedi is an Adjunct Professor at Heinz College with over twenty years of experience leading global projects in IT services. She specializes in teaching software engineering and computer science courses, including object-oriented design, data structures, algorithms, and applying software engineering best practices to data science projects.

name: Keith Eich
bio: Keith is an experienced media and technology executive with over 20 years in the industry, including leadership roles at NBCUniversal overseeing digital distribution and major deals like Hulu. He has transitioned across sectors including legal tech and currently serves as an elected official while maintaining connections to Carnegie Mellon.

name: Laurie Ellington
title: Adjunct Instructor
bio: Laurie Ellington is a Public Policy Manager at Amazon.com working on federal issues including immigration, education, and accessibility for people with disabilities. She brings extensive experience in legislative affairs, telecommunications policy, and industry coalition building from her roles at CTIA and in Congress.

name: Alison Emilio
title: Executive Director
bio: Alison Emilio is an entertainment executive with over 25 years of experience in marketing, communications, and strategic partnerships across film, television, and nonprofit sectors. She currently leads WORDTheatre and previously directed ReFrame, a Sundance Institute and Women In Film Los Angeles initiative advancing gender equity in the entertainment industry.

name: Dennis Epple
title: Thomas Lord University Professor of Economics
bio: Dennis Epple is the Thomas Lord University Professor of Economics at Carnegie Mellon University, where he conducts research in economics and public policy. His work focuses on understanding economic systems and their implications for policy design.

name: Laura Zwicker Everhart
title: Director of Development
bio: Laura is an experienced development officer with over a decade of experience fundraising for multiple organizations. She manages fundraising initiatives and six- and seven-figure gifts while collaborating with university-wide development goals.

name: Margaret (Mimi) Priselac Falbo
title: Adjunct Faculty
bio: Dr. Falbo is a consultant in health care quality and process improvement with 35 years of healthcare experience, including 23 years in management roles. She specializes in organizational transformation using Toyota principles and developing low-cost, high-quality, community-integrated healthcare programs.

name: Trevor Filipiak
title: Analytics & Business Intelligence Consultant
bio: Trevor Filipiak is an Analytics & Business Intelligence Consultant with expertise across the full lifecycle of analytics, from data ingestion and transformation through to dashboard development and predictive modeling. He brings experience in consulting, government contracting, and software/SaaS sectors with certifications in Tableau and Alteryx.

name: Gary Franko
title: Adjunct Faculty and Senior Multimedia Designer
bio: Gary Franko is an adjunct faculty member at Heinz College who specializes in multimedia design and media communication. He teaches courses in multimedia and media & communication design, bringing practical industry expertise to student projects.

name: Erin Franzman
title: Advisor
bio: Erin Franzman is an advisor at Social Dept AI, bringing automation to entertainment marketing teams. With extensive experience as Vice President of Digital and Social Strategy at CBS, she has guided digital transformation and marketing initiatives across major entertainment properties and networks.

name: Matthew Fredrikson
title: Assistant Professor
department: Computer Science Department & Institute for Software Research
bio: Matthew Fredrikson is an Assistant Professor in the School of Computer Science whose research focuses on security, privacy, formal methods, and programming languages. He is a member of CyLab, the Societal Computing Program, and the Principles of Programming Group.

name: Scott Garner
title: Adjunct Professor
bio: Scott Garner is a senior programming and brand strategist with 25 years of experience in content curation, marketing, and research for Fortune 500 companies. His research focuses on media psychology, audience engagement across traditional and digital platforms, and diversity, equity, and inclusion in media.

name: Madeline Gent
title: Adjunct Faculty
bio: Madeline Gent is the Executive Director of Associated Artists of Pittsburgh, a 112-year-old nonprofit organization dedicated to exhibiting and supporting Pittsburgh-based artists. With expertise in art history and experience at major cultural institutions including the Smithsonian and University of Maryland, she brings deep knowledge of arts administration and visual culture to her work.

name: Robert Gradeck
title: Adjunct Faculty
department: Heinz College of Information Systems and Public Policy

name: Hayley Haldeman
title: Adjunct Faculty
department: Heinz College of Information Systems and Public Policy
bio: Hayley Haldeman is an attorney and nonprofit leader with a decade of hands-on experience in nonprofit strategy and administration, particularly with arts organizations. She currently serves as Chief Administrative Officer of the Pittsburgh Cultural Trust and teaches courses on law and the arts at Carnegie Mellon.

name: Matt Hannigan
title: Adjunct Faculty
department: Heinz College of Information Systems and Public Policy
bio: Matt Hannigan is co-founder and Deputy Director of The Sprout Fund, a nonprofit supporting innovative community projects in Pittsburgh that has invested nearly $4 million across 450+ projects.
